events.text: The patient is a 66-year-old female named Margaret Osei-Bonsu. She has a past medical history significant for type 2 diabetes mellitus (diet-controlled), hypertension, and a 15-pack-year smoking history (quit 10 years prior). She has no family history of pancreatic cancer. She is of Ghanaian descent and works as a retired librarian.

At age 66 years, the patient presented to her primary care physician with a 10-week history of progressive painless jaundice, 14-pound unintentional weight loss, new-onset epigastric discomfort radiating to the back, and pale, greasy stools. On review of systems she also reported pruritus and dark urine. Physical examination revealed scleral icterus, mild hepatomegaly, and a palpable gallbladder (Courvoisier sign). Laboratory evaluation showed total bilirubin 8.2 mg/dL, direct bilirubin 6.9 mg/dL, alkaline phosphatase 420 U/L, AST 98 U/L, ALT 112 U/L, CA 19-9 1,204 U/mL, CEA 7.1 ng/mL, and albumin 3.1 g/dL. She was referred urgently to gastroenterology and hepatobiliary surgery.

At age 66 years, the patient had a CT abdomen and pelvis with triple-phase contrast protocol (pancreatic protocol CT), which showed a 3.8 cm hypoenhancing mass in the head and uncinate process of the pancreas causing upstream pancreatic ductal dilation to 7 mm and common bile duct dilation to 16 mm. The mass abutted the superior mesenteric artery (SMA) over 180 degrees of its circumference and involved the superior mesenteric vein (SMV) with focal narrowing. No encasement of the celiac axis or hepatic artery was seen. No discrete hepatic or peritoneal metastases were identified. Two sub-centimeter peripancreatic lymph nodes were noted (largest 0.9 cm). Findings were consistent with borderline resectable pancreatic head mass by AHPBA/SSO/SSAT criteria.

At age 66 years, the patient had an MRI abdomen with MRCP, which confirmed the 3.8 cm pancreatic head mass with SMA abutment (contact over approximately 170 degrees) and SMV involvement with venous narrowing but without complete occlusion. No hepatic lesions. No ascites. The main pancreatic duct measured 6.5 mm in the body. Findings corroborated borderline resectable status. No distant metastatic disease was identified.

At age 66 years, the patient had an endoscopic ultrasound (EUS)-guided fine needle biopsy of the pancreatic head mass. Pathology revealed moderately differentiated pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (PDAC). Tumor cells were arranged in infiltrative glandular structures with desmoplastic stroma. Immunohistochemistry showed positivity for CK7, CK19, MUC1, and CA 19-9, and negativity for CK20 and CDX2. The Ki-67 proliferation index was approximately 35%. No lymphovascular invasion was identified in the biopsy specimen. The morphology was consistent with conventional pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma, excluding acinar cell carcinoma and neuroendocrine tumor.

At age 66 years, the patient received a diagnosis of borderline resectable pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma of the pancreatic head and uncinate process, clinical stage T3 N1 M0 (AJCC 8th edition, Stage IIB). Primary site: Pancreatic head (C25.0). Histology: Adenocarcinoma, NOS (8140/3). Summary stage: Regional by direct extension (SMA abutment) and regional lymph nodes involved (peripancreatic adenopathy). Site-specific factors: tumor size 3.8 cm, SMA abutment without encasement, SMV involvement with narrowing, biliary obstruction present, CA 19-9 1,204 U/mL, CEA 7.1 ng/mL. Performance status ECOG 1. Confirmation: Positive histology (EUS-guided biopsy).

At age 66 years, the patient was discussed at the multidisciplinary pancreatic tumor board. Given borderline resectable disease with SMA abutment and SMV involvement, consensus recommendation was for neoadjuvant systemic chemotherapy with FOLFIRINOX (oxaliplatin, irinotecan, leucovorin, fluorouracil) followed by reassessment for chemoradiation and surgical resection. ERCP with biliary stent placement was recommended prior to initiation of chemotherapy given significant obstructive jaundice (bilirubin 8.2 mg/dL). Performance status was deemed adequate for doublet or triplet regimen. Patient expressed strong preference to proceed with aggressive curative-intent therapy and consented to the treatment plan.

At age 66 years, the patient underwent ERCP with placement of a fully covered self-expanding metallic biliary stent (FCSEMS) for palliation of malignant biliary obstruction. Procedure was uncomplicated. Post-procedure bilirubin declined to 1.8 mg/dL within two weeks, and liver function tests normalized, allowing safe initiation of chemotherapy.

At age 66 years, the patient initiated neoadjuvant FOLFIRINOX (modified): oxaliplatin 85 mg/m2, leucovorin 400 mg/m2, irinotecan 150 mg/m2 (reduced from 180 mg/m2 due to biliary stent and Gilbert syndrome concern), and 5-fluorouracil 2400 mg/m2 as a 46-hour continuous infusion, administered every 14 days. Neulasta (pegfilgrastim) prophylaxis given with each cycle. Treatment intent: neoadjuvant (curative). Cycles 1 through 8 planned.

At age 66 years, during neoadjuvant FOLFIRINOX (cycles 1-3), the patient developed grade 2 peripheral sensory neuropathy affecting the fingertips and toes (CTCAE v5 grade 2), grade 2 fatigue, grade 1 nausea adequately controlled with ondansetron and prochlorperazine, and grade 1 alopecia. She also experienced one episode of grade 2 diarrhea during cycle 2 that was managed with loperamide and temporary dose hold. No febrile neutropenia occurred. CA 19-9 after cycle 2 had declined to 580 U/mL. Dose reductions were not required.

At age 66 years, after 4 cycles of neoadjuvant FOLFIRINOX, the patient had a restaging CT abdomen/pelvis with pancreatic protocol. The pancreatic head mass had decreased to 3.0 cm (from 3.8 cm, representing a 21% decrease in maximal diameter). The degree of SMA abutment had decreased to approximately 120 degrees. The SMV narrowing had improved. No new hepatic or peritoneal lesions. CA 19-9 at this timepoint was 310 U/mL. Findings were consistent with partial response per RECIST 1.1. Recommendation was to continue FOLFIRINOX for an additional 4 cycles.

At age 66 years, during neoadjuvant FOLFIRINOX (cycles 5-8), the patient's grade 2 peripheral neuropathy progressed to grade 3 (interfering with activities of daily living), prompting discontinuation of oxaliplatin after cycle 6. She completed cycles 7 and 8 with fluorouracil/leucovorin/irinotecan (FOLFIRI) backbone only. Grade 2 fatigue persisted. She lost an additional 6 pounds during this phase. No hospitalizations were required.

At age 66 years, after completing 8 cycles of neoadjuvant chemotherapy (6 cycles FOLFIRINOX, 2 cycles FOLFIRI), restaging CT abdomen/pelvis with pancreatic protocol demonstrated the primary pancreatic head mass measuring 2.6 cm (down from 3.8 cm at baseline), with SMA abutment now less than 90 degrees. The SMV appeared patent without significant narrowing. No distant metastatic disease. CA 19-9 was 85 U/mL. Overall response assessment: partial response. The multidisciplinary tumor board reviewed the case and recommended proceeding with chemoradiation prior to surgical resection to further sterilize surgical margins and address potential micrometastatic disease.

At age 66 years, the patient was presented again at the pancreatic tumor board following completion of 8 cycles of neoadjuvant chemotherapy. CA 19-9 had declined from 1,204 to 85 U/mL (93% decline). Imaging demonstrated partial response with reduction in SMA abutment to less than 90 degrees and SMV patency restored. Peripheral neuropathy had improved to grade 1 over the 4-week break. Performance status ECOG 1. The board recommended consolidation chemoradiation with capecitabine as a radiosensitizer, followed by surgical resection (Whipple procedure/pancreaticoduodenectomy) approximately 4-6 weeks after completion of chemoradiation. Patient agreed to proceed.

At age 66 years, the patient received stereotactic body radiation therapy (SBRT) to the primary pancreatic tumor and regional nodal stations. Total dose: 54 Gy in 28 fractions (1.8 Gy/fraction) using IMRT technique with simultaneous integrated boost (SIB) to the gross tumor volume. Concurrent capecitabine 825 mg/m2 twice daily on radiation days was administered as a radiosensitizer. Radiation fields included the primary tumor with 1 cm margin, celiac axis nodal region, and peripancreatic lymph nodes. Treatment duration: 5.5 weeks. Stereotactic image guidance (CBCT) used daily. The patient tolerated treatment reasonably well with grade 1 nausea, grade 1 radiation dermatitis, and mild fatigue.

At age 66 years, during chemoradiation, the patient experienced grade 2 nausea and anorexia requiring antiemetic intensification with olanzapine 5 mg nightly added to ondansetron. She lost an additional 4 pounds. Grade 1 radiation-induced dermatitis over the epigastric region was managed with topical emollients. Grade 1 diarrhea occurred during weeks 3-4, self-limited with dietary modification. No treatment interruptions were required. Capecitabine hand-foot syndrome was grade 1 only.

At age 66 years, approximately 5 weeks after completing chemoradiation, the patient underwent repeat CT abdomen/pelvis with pancreatic protocol for pre-surgical assessment. The pancreatic head mass measured 2.3 cm with further reduction in SMA abutment (estimated contact less than 60 degrees). The SMV was patent. No new hepatic lesions or peritoneal implants were identified. CA 19-9 was 52 U/mL. The surgical team deemed the patient a candidate for attempted curative resection with Whipple procedure. PET-CT was additionally obtained showing no FDG-avid distant metastases, with moderate FDG uptake in the primary pancreatic mass (SUVmax 4.1, decreased from prior).

At age 66 years, the patient underwent open pylorus-preserving pancreaticoduodenectomy (PPPD, Whipple procedure) with en bloc superior mesenteric vein (SMV) segmental resection and primary venous reconstruction. Intraoperatively, the tumor was found to be densely adherent to the SMV over a 2 cm segment requiring resection with end-to-end anastomosis. The SMA was carefully dissected and preserved with adequate margins. Total operative time was 7 hours and 40 minutes. Estimated blood loss was 820 mL; the patient received 2 units of packed red blood cells intraoperatively. A pylorus-preserving reconstruction was achieved with hepaticojejunostomy, pancreaticojejunostomy (duct-to-mucosa), and duodenojejunostomy. Intraoperative frozen sections of the pancreatic neck margin were negative. The common bile duct margin and portal vein margin were also negative on frozen section.

At age 66 years, the patient had the surgical resection specimen evaluated by anatomic pathology. Final pathology of the Whipple resection specimen: moderately differentiated pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma, 2.1 cm in greatest dimension, involving the pancreatic head and uncinate process. Extensive treatment effect (fibrosis, calcifications, necrosis) with College of American Pathologists (CAP) tumor regression grade 2 (moderate response: residual cancer with evident tumor regression, but more than single cells or rare small groups of cancer cells). Lymphovascular invasion: absent. Perineural invasion: present. Margins: pancreatic neck margin negative (clearance 3 mm), common bile duct margin negative (clearance 5 mm), retroperitoneal/uncinate margin (SMA margin) negative (clearance 1.2 mm), SMV margin negative (clearance 2 mm) following vascular resection. Total lymph nodes examined: 22; metastatic carcinoma in 1 of 22 lymph nodes (1/22). Pathologic stage: ypT3 N1 M0 (AJCC 8th edition, Stage IIB). R0 resection confirmed.

At age 66 years, the patient had next-generation sequencing (NGS) performed on the resected Whipple specimen using a comprehensive genomic profiling panel (tumor tissue, 648-gene panel). Specimen: primary pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (pancreatic head, surgical resection). Tumor mutational burden: 3 mutations/Mb (low). Microsatellite status: microsatellite stable (MSS). Copy number: no high-level amplifications. Loss of heterozygosity (LOH): low. Actionable alterations: KRAS p.G12V mutation (variant allele frequency 42%, pathogenic), TP53 p.R175H mutation (VAF 38%, pathogenic). Additional alterations of potential significance: SMAD4 loss (homozygous deletion, likely driver), CDKN2A/p16 homozygous deletion. Other detected alterations: ARID1A p.Q556* truncating mutation (VAF 18%), KDM6A missense variant of uncertain significance. Fusions: none detected. NTRK1/2/3 fusion: not detected. BRCA1/2: no pathogenic mutations detected (germline testing recommended given family history evaluation - result: germline BRCA1/2 negative confirmed on separate germline assay). ATM: no pathogenic mutation. PALB2: no pathogenic mutation. HER2 amplification: not detected. PD-L1 expression by IHC: 1% (low). Clinical interpretation: KRAS G12V-mutant, TP53-mutant, SMAD4-deleted, CDKN2A-deleted pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma; microsatellite stable, low TMB; no currently actionable targets for approved targeted therapies; germline testing negative.

At age 66 years, on postoperative day 5 following the Whipple procedure, the patient developed a pancreatic fistula (postoperative pancreatic fistula, POPF). Drain amylase on postoperative day 3 was 4,280 U/L (greater than 3x upper limit of normal serum amylase), consistent with International Study Group on Pancreatic Fistula (ISGPF) Grade B pancreatic fistula. The patient required prolonged drain maintenance, NPO status with transition to total parenteral nutrition (TPN), and octreotide 200 mcg subcutaneously three times daily. She was ultimately discharged home on postoperative day 14 with Jackson-Pratt drain in situ and outpatient wound care. The drain was removed at 6 weeks post-surgery after confirmation of fistula closure by sinogram.

At age 66 years (approximately 10 weeks post-surgery), the patient was seen in follow-up by medical oncology. She had recovered from the pancreatic fistula with drain removed 2 weeks prior. Performance status ECOG 1. She had regained 4 pounds. She reported persistent grade 1 peripheral neuropathy in the fingertips (attributed to prior oxaliplatin). She had developed new-onset exocrine pancreatic insufficiency requiring pancreatic enzyme replacement therapy (PERT) with pancrelipase 72,000 units with meals. Her pre-existing type 2 diabetes had worsened post-pancreatectomy and now required metformin plus basal insulin glargine. CA 19-9 was 18 U/mL (within normal limits). CEA was 2.4 ng/mL. Discussion of adjuvant chemotherapy was initiated: recommendation for gemcitabine plus capecitabine (GemCap regimen) per the ESPAC-4 trial evidence.

At age 67 years (approximately 12 weeks post-surgery), the patient initiated adjuvant gemcitabine plus capecitabine (GemCap). Gemcitabine 1000 mg/m2 intravenously on days 1, 8, and 15 of each 28-day cycle; capecitabine 1660 mg/m2/day orally in two divided doses on days 1-21 of each 28-day cycle. Treatment intent: adjuvant (curative). Planned duration: 6 cycles (24 weeks). Dose reductions applied: capecitabine started at 80% dose (1330 mg/m2/day) given prior chemoradiation, post-surgical debility, and ongoing neuropathy concern.

At age 67 years, during adjuvant GemCap (cycles 1-3), the patient experienced recurrence of the pancreatic fistula (Grade A, minor leak) detected by rising drain output on cycle 1 day 10. Gemcitabine was temporarily held for 2 weeks. The leak resolved spontaneously with conservative management (clear liquids, drain irrigation, octreotide). Capecitabine caused grade 2 hand-foot syndrome (palmar-plantar erythrodysesthesia) requiring dose reduction to 75% (1247 mg/m2/day) and topical urea cream. Grade 2 fatigue and grade 1 nausea were also present. Gemcitabine-associated cytopenias: grade 2 thrombocytopenia (nadir platelet count 68,000/mcL) and grade 2 anemia (hemoglobin nadir 8.9 g/dL) requiring gemcitabine day 15 dose omission in cycles 2 and 3.

At age 67 years, after completing 3 cycles of adjuvant GemCap, the patient had a restaging evaluation. CA 19-9 remained at 22 U/mL (stable, normal range). CEA 2.1 ng/mL. Performance status ECOG 1-2. The fistula recurrence had resolved. Gemcitabine was dose-reduced to 75% (750 mg/m2) due to cumulative myelosuppression. Capecitabine dose was maintained at 75%. The patient expressed significant fatigue and desire to reduce treatment intensity, but agreed to complete the planned 6 cycles. Discussion of treatment goals and quality of life was documented. Diabetes management was ongoing with endocrinology co-management.

At age 67 years, during adjuvant GemCap cycles 4-6, the patient developed grade 1 peripheral neuropathy worsening (now grade 2, attributed to additive capecitabine neurotoxicity on background oxaliplatin-induced neuropathy). Grade 2 fatigue continued. Grade 1 mucositis occurred during cycle 5. Platelet counts recovered to above 100,000/mcL with dose-reduced gemcitabine. No further episodes of fistula leak. The patient completed all 6 cycles of adjuvant GemCap, with cycles 4-6 administered at reduced doses (gemcitabine 75%, capecitabine 75%). Final CA 19-9 at end of adjuvant treatment: 19 U/mL.

At age 67 years, upon completion of adjuvant GemCap (approximately 8 months post-surgery), the patient had surveillance CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast. No evidence of recurrent or metastatic disease. The surgical bed showed expected post-operative changes with fibrosis and no suspicious soft tissue masses. The liver was without focal lesions. No peritoneal implants. No adenopathy above 1 cm. CA 19-9 was 19 U/mL. CEA 2.0 ng/mL. Assessment: no evidence of disease (NED). Plan for surveillance imaging every 3-4 months for the first 2 years.

At age 67 years (approximately 14 months post-Whipple), the patient was seen for routine surveillance. She reported improved energy and had regained her pre-diagnosis weight. Grade 1 peripheral neuropathy in the fingertips persisted but was stable and not worsening. Pancreatic enzyme replacement was optimized. Blood glucose control had improved with endocrinology adjustments to insulin regimen. CA 19-9 was 24 U/mL. CEA 2.2 ng/mL. Performance status ECOG 1. The patient resumed volunteer work at the local library and reported good quality of life. She was counseled on the importance of ongoing surveillance and surveillance CT was scheduled in 3 months.

At age 67 years (approximately 17 months post-Whipple), routine surveillance CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast showed a new 1.4 cm hypodense lesion in liver segment VI, with imaging characteristics that could represent a metastatic deposit (rim-enhancing, round, without features of a cyst or hemangioma). No other new lesions. CA 19-9 had risen to 89 U/mL (from a nadir of 18 U/mL). CEA 4.8 ng/mL. Findings were concerning for hepatic metastasis and prompted urgent MRI liver for further characterization.

At age 67 years, MRI liver with hepatobiliary contrast agent (gadoxetate disodium) was performed for characterization of the hepatic lesion. The 1.4 cm segment VI lesion demonstrated hypointensity on hepatobiliary phase, restricted diffusion, and arterial rim enhancement with washout, features diagnostic of metastatic disease to the liver. No additional hepatic lesions were seen. No extrahepatic disease on concurrent CT chest. Findings confirmed solitary liver metastasis consistent with recurrent pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma. CA 19-9 at this time: 89 U/mL.

At age 67 years, the patient underwent CT-guided percutaneous core needle biopsy of the segment VI hepatic lesion for histologic confirmation. Pathology: metastatic adenocarcinoma consistent with pancreatic primary origin. Immunohistochemistry: CK7 positive, CK19 positive, MUC1 positive, CA 19-9 positive, CK20 negative, CDX2 negative, TTF-1 negative, confirming pancreatobiliary origin. Morphology was consistent with the known primary pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma. NGS on the metastatic biopsy confirmed KRAS p.G12V, TP53 p.R175H, and SMAD4 loss, concordant with the primary tumor profile. No new actionable alterations acquired. Microsatellite stable confirmed. BRCA1/2 somatic mutation: absent.

At age 67 years, the patient was presented at the multidisciplinary tumor board following confirmation of solitary hepatic metastasis from pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma, 17 months after curative-intent Whipple procedure. Disease-free interval was 17 months. Performance status ECOG 1. CA 19-9 89 U/mL. Liver function tests were normal. The board discussed oligometastatic disease management. Surgical resection of the liver metastasis was considered but deemed high-risk given prior Whipple, and complete surgical clearance with negative margins was felt uncertain. SBRT to the hepatic lesion was discussed. The consensus recommendation was to initiate systemic chemotherapy for metastatic pancreatic cancer (gemcitabine/nab-paclitaxel, as the patient had previously received FOLFIRINOX and GemCap in the curative setting), with restaging after 2-3 cycles to assess response and oligometastatic status before considering locoregional therapy. Patient was counseled regarding goals of care transition to disease control, and she agreed to proceed.

At age 67 years, the patient initiated first-line systemic therapy for metastatic pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma with gemcitabine 1000 mg/m2 and nab-paclitaxel 125 mg/m2 on days 1, 8, and 15 of each 28-day cycle. Treatment intent: palliative (disease control, metastatic setting). Gemcitabine/nab-paclitaxel was selected given prior exposure to FOLFIRINOX-based and fluoropyrimidine-based regimens in the neoadjuvant and adjuvant settings. Granulocyte colony-stimulating factor (G-CSF) prophylaxis was not started initially but held as rescue therapy.

documents.text:
INITIAL EVALUATION NOTE — PRIMARY CARE / URGENT REFERRAL

ATTENDING PHYSICIAN: Internal Medicine / Primary Care
DEPARTMENT: General Internal Medicine
VISIT TYPE: Office Visit — Urgent

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT:
Progressive jaundice, unintentional weight loss, epigastric pain radiating to the back, and pale greasy stools over the past 10 weeks.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:
Ms. Margaret Osei-Bonsu is a 66-year-old Ghanaian-American woman, retired librarian, who presents today with a 10-week history of what she initially attributed to "stomach trouble" but which has progressively worsened to include painless jaundice, significant unintentional weight loss, and new epigastric discomfort. She reports that family members first noticed yellowing of her eyes approximately 8 weeks ago, though she herself did not seek evaluation at that time, hoping it would resolve on its own. Over the past 3 weeks, the jaundice has become more pronounced and she has noted darkening of her urine (described as "cola-colored") and pale, greasy, malodorous stools that float in the toilet. She endorses diffuse pruritus, particularly bothersome at night, which she initially attributed to dry skin.

She reports a 14-pound unintentional weight loss over the past 2 months (current weight 138 lbs, prior baseline approximately 152 lbs). She has had markedly decreased appetite, describing early satiety and nausea without vomiting. She denies frank hematemesis or melena, though stool color has been pale as described above.

The epigastric discomfort is described as a dull, aching pressure, rated 4-5/10 at baseline, with radiation to the mid-back. Pain is not clearly related to food intake but seems slightly worse after eating fatty meals. She denies any acute severe pain episodes. She has not taken any NSAIDs or new over-the-counter medications.

She denies fever or chills. She has not had prior episodes of pancreatitis, cholelithiasis, or biliary colic. She has never had an ERCP or prior abdominal surgery. She denies alcohol use. She reports mild fatigue that has worsened over the past several weeks, making it difficult to maintain her usual activities.

She denies any recent travel, sick contacts, or known hepatitis exposure. She has had no prior liver disease, hepatitis B or C infection, or autoimmune hepatitis.

Given the constellation of findings — painless obstructive jaundice, significant weight loss, new-onset back pain, and steatorrhea — there is high clinical concern for a pancreatic or biliary malignancy and urgent further workup has been initiated.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS:
Constitutional: Significant fatigue, 14-pound unintentional weight loss over approximately 8 weeks, decreased appetite, early satiety. Denies fever, chills, or night sweats.
Gastrointestinal: Epigastric discomfort radiating to the back as described. Pale, greasy, floating stools. Dark urine. Nausea without vomiting. Denies hematemesis, melena, or hematochezia. Denies change in bowel frequency. No known history of ulcer disease.
Hepatic/Biliary: Scleral icterus and skin jaundice present 8 weeks. Generalized pruritus.
Musculoskeletal: Mild generalized weakness. Denies arthralgias or myalgias.
Neurological: Denies confusion, headache, focal weakness, or numbness.
Cardiovascular: Denies chest pain, palpitations, orthopnea, or lower extremity edema.
Respiratory: Denies cough, shortness of breath, or hemoptysis.
Endocrine: Known type 2 diabetes mellitus, diet-controlled; no recent significant changes in glucose monitoring though she notes she has been eating less. Hypertension, stable on medications.
Dermatologic: Generalized pruritus as noted; no rash.
Genitourinary: Dark urine as described. Denies dysuria or hematuria.

---

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY:
1. Type 2 diabetes mellitus — diet-controlled; HbA1c last checked approximately 12 months ago at 6.7%.
2. Hypertension — on amlodipine 5 mg daily; well controlled.
3. Former smoker — 15 pack-year history; quit approximately 10 years ago.
4. No history of pancreatitis, cholelithiasis, or prior abdominal surgery.
5. No prior malignancy.
6. Colonoscopy 5 years ago — no polyps identified.
7. Mammography and Pap smears up to date per primary care records — unremarkable.

PAST SURGICAL HISTORY:
No prior abdominal or thoracic surgeries. Cesarean section approximately 30 years ago (remote history).

FAMILY HISTORY:
Mother: deceased, cause unknown (died in Ghana, limited records available).
Father: deceased, hypertension and stroke.
Siblings: two sisters, one brother — no known malignancies reported by the patient.
No known family history of pancreatic cancer, breast cancer, ovarian cancer, or colorectal cancer. No known family history of BRCA-related or Lynch syndrome-related cancers. The patient acknowledges limited knowledge of extended family medical history given distance and communication barriers.

SOCIAL HISTORY:
Marital status: Widowed (husband deceased 5 years ago, cardiac event).
Living situation: Lives alone in a single-story home; two adult children locally who are actively involved.
Occupation: Retired librarian; volunteers part-time at the local public library.
Ethnicity: Ghanaian descent; emigrated to the United States approximately 35 years ago.
Alcohol: Denies alcohol use.
Tobacco: Former smoker, 15 pack-year history, quit 10 years ago.
Illicit substances: Denies.
Diet: Primarily Ghanaian/West African dietary patterns; notes recent marked decrease in oral intake.
Exercise: Previously walked daily; activity significantly curtailed over the past 6-8 weeks due to fatigue.
Functional status: ECOG Performance Status 1 — ambulatory, capable of all self-care, unable to carry out work activities; up and about more than 50% of waking hours.
Health literacy: High; engaged patient who asks thoughtful questions.

ALLERGIES:
No known drug allergies (NKDA).

MEDICATIONS:
1. Amlodipine 5 mg orally once daily (hypertension)
2. Multivitamin 1 tablet orally once daily
3. No anticoagulants, antiplatelet agents, or herbal supplements reported
(Patient is not on metformin or any hypoglycemic agents — diabetes managed with diet alone at this visit)

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:
General: Alert, well-groomed, moderately ill-appearing woman in no acute distress. Appears thin and slightly fatigued. Speech is clear and cooperative.
Vital Signs: Temperature 98.4°F (36.9°C), blood pressure 136/82 mmHg, heart rate 78 bpm (regular), respiratory rate 16 breaths/min, oxygen saturation 97% on room air. Weight 138 lbs (62.6 kg), BMI 22.4 kg/m2 (down from reported baseline BMI ~24.5).
Skin: Diffuse icterus of the skin. Mild excoriation marks on bilateral forearms and torso consistent with chronic pruritus. No spider angiomata or palmar erythema. No petechiae or ecchymosis.
Head, Eyes, Ears, Nose, Throat: Scleral icterus bilaterally, moderately prominent. No conjunctival pallor. Oral mucosa moist. No oral lesions or lymphadenopathy in the head and neck.
Lymph Nodes: No cervical, supraclavicular, or axillary lymphadenopathy palpated. No palpable left supraclavicular (Virchow's) node.
Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. Normal S1 and S2. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops. No lower extremity edema.
Respiratory: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezes, rhonchi, or crackles.
Abdomen:
  — Abdomen mildly distended in the epigastrium.
  — Soft overall; mild tenderness to deep palpation in the epigastric region and right upper quadrant without peritoneal signs (no guarding or rebound).
  — Hepatomegaly present: liver edge palpable approximately 4 cm below the right costal margin in the midclavicular line; smooth, non-tender edge.
  — A non-tender, smooth, palpable mass is noted in the right upper quadrant just below the liver edge, consistent with a distended gallbladder (Courvoisier sign).
  — No splenomegaly appreciated.
  — No ascites clinically (no fluid wave, shifting dullness absent).
  — Bowel sounds normoactive.
Musculoskeletal: No focal bony tenderness. No joint swelling. Mild generalized muscle wasting noted in bilateral upper and lower extremities.
Neurological: Alert and oriented x4. Cranial nerves II-XII grossly intact. Motor strength 5/5 in all extremities. Sensation intact. No asterixis.
Extremities: No clubbing, cyanosis, or edema. Peripheral pulses 2+ bilaterally.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS:
Comprehensive Metabolic Panel:
  — Sodium: 138 mEq/L
  — Potassium: 4.0 mEq/L
  — Chloride: 101 mEq/L
  — Bicarbonate: 24 mEq/L
  — BUN: 14 mg/dL
  — Creatinine: 0.82 mg/dL (eGFR >60 mL/min/1.73m2)
  — Glucose: 142 mg/dL (fasting)
  — Total bilirubin: 8.2 mg/dL (H)
  — Direct (conjugated) bilirubin: 6.9 mg/dL (H)
  — Indirect bilirubin: 1.3 mg/dL
  — Alkaline phosphatase (ALP): 420 U/L (H)
  — AST: 98 U/L (H)
  — ALT: 112 U/L (H)
  — GGT: 312 U/L (H)
  — Total protein: 6.4 g/dL
  — Albumin: 3.1 g/dL (L)
  — LDH: 198 U/L (normal)
Complete Blood Count:
  — WBC: 7,200/mcL (normal differential)
  — Hemoglobin: 11.4 g/dL (L) — mild normocytic anemia
  — Hematocrit: 34%
  — MCV: 88 fL
  — Platelets: 342,000/mcL
Coagulation:
  — PT/INR: 1.2 (mildly elevated, consistent with mild hepatic synthetic dysfunction or nutritional vitamin K deficiency secondary to biliary obstruction)
Tumor Markers:
  — CA 19-9: 1,204 U/mL (markedly elevated; reference range <37 U/mL)
  — CEA: 7.1 ng/mL (mildly elevated; reference range <3.0 ng/mL)
Urinalysis: Bilirubin 3+ (conjugated bilirubinuria), urobilinogen absent, consistent with complete or near-complete biliary obstruction.

---

IMAGING RESULTS (reviewed at this visit):
No imaging has been performed at this time. A pancreatic protocol CT abdomen and pelvis with triple-phase intravenous contrast has been ordered urgently and is being arranged for completion within the next 24-48 hours. An abdominal ultrasound was considered as a screening study but given the high pre-test probability for pancreatic pathology, a dedicated pancreatic protocol CT is being ordered directly as the more diagnostically informative initial cross-sectional imaging study.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN:

ASSESSMENT:
Ms. Osei-Bonsu is a 66-year-old woman with diet-controlled type 2 diabetes and hypertension who presents with a highly concerning constellation of findings: 10-week progressive painless obstructive jaundice with direct hyperbilirubinemia, significant unintentional weight loss of 14 pounds, epigastric pain radiating to the back, steatorrhea, and markedly elevated CA 19-9 at 1,204 U/mL. The presence of a palpable gallbladder (Courvoisier sign) in the setting of painless jaundice is a classic finding pointing toward malignant biliary obstruction at or distal to the common bile duct — the pancreatic head is the most likely primary site. The degree of CA 19-9 elevation (>30x the upper limit of normal) combined with the clinical picture is highly concerning for pancreatic malignancy, most likely pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma. Choledocholithiasis alone is an unlikely explanation given the absence of prior biliary symptoms, the degree of weight loss, the degree of CA 19-9 elevation, and the presence of the Courvoisier sign.

Differential diagnosis includes:
1. Pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (primary concern, most likely)
2. Distal cholangiocarcinoma (biliary tree primary)
3. Ampullary carcinoma
4. Duodenal carcinoma
5. Autoimmune pancreatitis / IgG4-related disease (less likely given marked weight loss and tumor marker elevation, but cannot be excluded without imaging and IgG4 level)
6. Benign biliary stricture (unlikely given clinical picture)

PLAN:

1. Urgent pancreatic protocol CT abdomen and pelvis with triple-phase IV contrast: Ordered today; to be completed within 24-48 hours. Patient instructed on importance of NOT missing this study.

2. Additional bloodwork ordered: IgG4 level (to evaluate for autoimmune pancreatitis as alternative diagnosis), hepatitis B surface antigen and hepatitis B core antibody (prior to any planned treatments), hepatitis C antibody, CA 125, amylase, lipase.

3. Urgent referral to Gastroenterology: Placed today for endoscopic evaluation (ERCP and/or EUS), both for diagnostic purposes (tissue sampling) and potential therapeutic biliary decompression. Given bilirubin of 8.2 mg/dL and a CA 19-9 of 1,204 U/mL, rapid evaluation is paramount. The gastroenterology colleague has been contacted directly by telephone and has agreed to see the patient within 48-72 hours.

4. Urgent referral to Hepatobiliary Surgery / Surgical Oncology: Placed concurrently given the possibility of surgically resectable or borderline resectable disease and the importance of early surgical evaluation. The pancreatic surgery team has been notified.

5. Referral to Medical Oncology: Placed for early involvement and coordination of multidisciplinary care.

6. Nutritional support: Patient counseled on high-calorie, high-protein diet. Referral to clinical dietitian placed. Given the degree of malnutrition and weight loss, early nutritional optimization is critical, particularly if surgery becomes a consideration.

7. Symptom management:
   — Pruritus: Cholestyramine 4 g orally twice daily prescribed for symptomatic relief of cholestatic pruritus while awaiting biliary decompression; patient counseled that this is temporary symptomatic management.
   — Pain: Acetaminophen 500 mg as needed for mild pain; patient counseled to avoid NSAIDs given elevated bilirubin and potential coagulopathy.

8. Glycemic monitoring: Patient counseled to monitor blood glucose more closely given illness-related hyperglycemia (fasting glucose 142 mg/dL today). Will reassess need for temporary pharmacologic management at follow-up.

9. Patient education and emotional support: Extensive time spent with the patient explaining the concern for possible pancreatic malignancy. She was informed of the diagnostic steps being taken and the importance of prompt evaluation. She was accompanied by her daughter today, who was present for the discussion. Patient and family were thoughtful and appropriately distressed but expressed their desire to understand the situation fully and proceed with workup expeditiously. Appropriate anticipatory guidance was provided. Social work referral offered; patient deferred but was given contact information.

10. Follow-up: Patient to return or be contacted within 48-72 hours with imaging results. Urgent communication pathway established with GI and surgical colleagues. If imaging demonstrates findings consistent with pancreatic malignancy with biliary obstruction, prompt ERCP/EUS with tissue sampling and biliary stenting will be arranged.

CT ABDOMEN AND PELVIS WITH CONTRAST — PANCREATIC PROTOCOL (TRIPLE-PHASE)

CLINICAL INDICATION:
66-year-old female with biopsy-confirmed moderately differentiated pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma of the head and uncinate process, initially staged as borderline resectable based on SMA abutment and SMV narrowing on prior cross-sectional imaging. Patient has undergone biliary decompression via ERCP with FCSEMS placement and has now completed 4 cycles of modified neoadjuvant FOLFIRINOX (oxaliplatin/irinotecan/leucovorin/5-FU). TECHNIQUE:
Multidetector CT of the abdomen and pelvis was performed using a dedicated pancreatic protocol with triple-phase contrast enhancement (late arterial/pancreatic parenchymal phase, portal venous phase, and delayed venous phase). IV contrast: Omnipaque 350, 120 mL administered via power injector at 4 mL/sec. Oral contrast: negative (water) per protocol. Coronal and sagittal multiplanar reformations generated. Slice thickness: 1 mm collimation in the pancreatic bed, 3 mm for the remainder of the abdomen and pelvis. Images reviewed on workstation with dedicated vascular post-processing software. Comparison made to prior pancreatic protocol CT performed at the time of initial staging and prior MRI/MRCP.

FINDINGS:

PANCREAS:
The primary mass in the pancreatic head and uncinate process has undergone interval decrease in size and now measures approximately 3.0 cm in its largest transaxial dimension (previously 3.8 cm on the initial staging CT). The lesion retains its characteristic hypoenhancing appearance relative to the surrounding pancreatic parenchyma on the pancreatic parenchymal phase, with minimal internal enhancement on all phases, consistent with the known desmoplastic, hypovascular nature of pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma. The margins of the mass remain ill-defined with spiculated borders blending into the adjacent soft tissue and peripancreatic fat. There is an evolving zone of stromal fibrosis/treatment-related change at the periphery of the lesion that is difficult to distinguish from viable tumor on CT alone. The mass involves the pancreatic head from its superior aspect extending inferiorly through the uncinate process.

SUPERIOR MESENTERIC ARTERY (SMA):
The degree of tumor contact with the SMA has diminished compared with the prior study. The mass continues to abut the right lateral and posterior aspect of the SMA; however, the arc of contact is now estimated at approximately 120 degrees of the vessel circumference, representing a meaningful reduction from the approximately 180-degree abutment documented on the staging study. There is no definitive evidence of arterial encasement. The SMA lumen appears patent and of normal caliber without intraluminal thrombus or wall irregularity. Fat plane between the tumor and SMA, though still attenuated, shows slight improvement along the superior margin compared to prior.

SUPERIOR MESENTERIC VEIN (SMV) AND PORTAL CONFLUENCE:
The previously described focal narrowing of the SMV at the site of tumor contact has improved. CELIAC AXIS AND HEPATIC ARTERY:
The celiac axis, common hepatic artery, and proper hepatic artery remain uninvolved by tumor, without evidence of abutment or encasement. The splenic artery is patent along its course. No peri-arterial soft tissue thickening.

COMMON BILE DUCT AND BILIARY SYSTEM:
A fully covered self-expanding metallic stent is again demonstrated traversing the common bile duct and extending through the ampulla, with adequate luminal patency on current examination. The intrahepatic bile ducts are decompressed bilaterally without evidence of upstream biliary dilatation. No periductal enhancement or biliary wall thickening suggestive of cholangitis is seen. The liver is normal in size and attenuation. No focal hepatic lesions are identified on any phase of contrast enhancement. No new hypodense or hypervascular nodules. The hepatic parenchyma enhances homogeneously without segmental abnormality. No biloma or fluid collection. The hepatic veins and inferior vena cava are patent.

SPLEEN:
Normal size and homogeneous enhancement. No splenic lesion or infarction.

ADRENAL GLANDS:
Bilateral adrenal glands are normal in morphology. No adrenal lesion.

KIDNEYS:
Bilateral kidneys are symmetric in size and show normal cortical enhancement and excretion. No hydronephrosis. No suspicious renal mass. A stable 5 mm hypodense cortical lesion in the right kidney upper pole is unchanged and consistent with a simple cyst.

AORTA AND MAJOR VESSELS:
The aorta and iliac vessels are patent without aneurysmal dilatation. Mild atherosclerotic calcification of the aorta and its branches noted, as before.

LYMPH NODES:
The previously identified peripancreatic lymph nodes in the periportal and superior mesenteric regions remain present. The largest node in the peripancreatic distribution measures approximately 0.8 cm in short axis (previously 0.9 cm), now appearing slightly smaller. No discrete new adenopathy is identified in the retroperitoneum, mesentery, or pelvis. PELVIS:
No pelvic adenopathy or suspicious lesions. The uterus, bilateral adnexa, and bladder are unremarkable. No pelvic free fluid.

BONE WINDOWS:
Visualized osseous structures are without suspicious lytic or blastic lesions. Degenerative changes in the lumbar spine, as before.

IMPRESSION:

1. INTERVAL DECREASE IN SIZE OF THE PRIMARY PANCREATIC HEAD AND UNCINATE PROCESS MASS: The mass has diminished from 3.8 cm to approximately 3.0 cm in maximal transaxial dimension following 4 cycles of modified neoadjuvant FOLFIRINOX, representing a meaningful reduction in tumor bulk. The lesion retains its characteristic hypoenhancing morphology.

2. IMPROVED RELATIONSHIP WITH THE SUPERIOR MESENTERIC ARTERY: The arc of contact between the tumor and the SMA has decreased to an estimated 120 degrees compared with approximately 180 degrees at initial staging. While the mass still abuts the SMA, there is no imaging evidence of frank encasement or luminal irregularity, and the degree of involvement has improved with treatment.

3. IMPROVED SMV PATENCY: The focal narrowing of the SMV previously noted at the site of tumor contact has resolved on the current examination, with the vessel now appearing patent and of near-normal caliber without hemodynamically significant stenosis.

6. PATENT BILIARY STENT WITH DECOMPRESSED INTRAHEPATIC BILIARY TREE.

7. STABLE SUB-CENTIMETER PERIPANCREATIC LYMPH NODE, SLIGHTLY REDUCED IN SIZE — indeterminate in the context of known malignancy.

Overall, the imaging findings reflect a favorable treatment response with reduction in tumor size, decrease in the degree of SMA contact, and restoration of SMV patency following neoadjuvant chemotherapy. The constellation of changes represents objective tumor regression compared to baseline. The patient's borderline resectable status based on vascular involvement has shown measurable improvement, though the SMA continues to be abutted and surgical resectability should be reassessed in the context of multidisciplinary evaluation and further treatment response.

CT ABDOMEN AND PELVIS WITH PANCREATIC PROTOCOL CONTRAST
INDICATION: 66-year-old female with biopsy-proven pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma of the head and uncinate process, borderline resectable at diagnosis. Status post 8 cycles of neoadjuvant chemotherapy (modified FOLFIRINOX x6 cycles followed by FOLFIRI x2 cycles secondary to dose-limiting peripheral neuropathy). Prior biliary stenting via ERCP with fully covered metallic stent in place. Restaging evaluation to assess treatment response and candidacy for chemoradiation and/or surgical resection.

COMPARISON: CT abdomen/pelvis with pancreatic protocol dated at time of mid-treatment restaging (post-cycle 4 FOLFIRINOX) and diagnostic CT abdomen/pelvis at initial presentation.

TECHNIQUE: Multidetector CT of the abdomen and pelvis was performed in three phases following IV administration of iohexol 350 mg/mL: non-contrast, pancreatic parenchymal phase (approximately 40 seconds), and portal venous phase (approximately 70 seconds). Thin-section axial images were reconstructed at 0.625 mm with multiplanar reformations in the coronal and sagittal planes. Oral contrast was not administered. The biliary stent produces minor beam-hardening artifact in the region of the common bile duct and pancreatic head, partially limiting evaluation in that region.

FINDINGS:

PANCREAS:
There is a hypoenhancing, ill-defined soft tissue mass centered in the pancreatic head and uncinate process, now measuring approximately 2.6 cm in greatest axial dimension (previously 3.0 cm on mid-treatment restaging CT and 3.8 cm at baseline). The mass remains hypoenhancing relative to the surrounding pancreatic parenchyma on both the pancreatic parenchymal phase and portal venous phase images. The overall configuration of the lesion has become somewhat less distinct at its margins compared to baseline, a finding that may reflect treatment-related fibrosis and desmoplastic response superimposed on residual viable tumor. No new satellite lesions are identified within the pancreatic parenchyma.

The main pancreatic duct in the body and tail demonstrates persistent but improved upstream dilation, now measuring approximately 5.5 mm in the body (previously 6.5 mm on MRI and approximately 7 mm at initial CT), consistent with partial relief of ductal obstruction. The pancreatic tail parenchyma shows mild volume loss and atrophy compared with the prior study, a finding consistent with chronic obstruction of the main pancreatic duct.

VASCULAR RELATIONSHIPS:
Superior mesenteric artery (SMA): The previously described abutment of the pancreatic mass upon the SMA has significantly diminished on the current study. On careful review of the axial and coronal reformatted images, the soft tissue interface between the mass and the SMA encompasses an arc of contact that appears to fall below 90 degrees of the vessel circumference, representing a meaningful improvement from approximately 120 degrees on mid-treatment restaging and approximately 180 degrees at the time of initial presentation. The fat plane between the mass and the SMA, while not fully reconstituted, appears wider than on prior studies, and there is no convincing evidence of frank encasement or luminal narrowing of the SMA. The SMA flows freely without hemodynamically significant stenosis.

Superior mesenteric vein (SMV): The previously noted focal narrowing of the SMV has substantially improved. The SMV is now patent throughout its course without evidence of significant luminal compromise. There is no longer convincing soft tissue cuffing of the SMV. The SMV-portal vein confluence is well opacified and patent. No portal venous thrombosis is identified. The previously described venous narrowing at the level of the uncinate process has resolved on the current examination.

Celiac axis: The celiac axis and its major branches (common hepatic artery, proper hepatic artery, splenic artery) are patent without evidence of mass encasement or arterial stenosis. No involvement of the gastroduodenal artery stump territory is seen.

BILIARY SYSTEM:
The previously placed fully covered self-expanding metallic biliary stent is identified within the common bile duct, appearing intact with no evidence of migration. The intrahepatic bile ducts are mildly prominent bilaterally (right greater than left), but notably less dilated than at initial presentation. The common bile duct proximal to the stent shows minimal residual dilation, measuring approximately 5 mm at the hepatic hilum. This is improved compared with the initial common bile duct dilation of 16 mm, reflecting adequate biliary drainage through the indwelling stent.

LIVER:
The liver is mildly enlarged with a smooth contour. The hepatic parenchyma demonstrates homogeneous enhancement without focal hypodense or hyperenhancing lesions identified in either the arterial or portal venous phase. No subcapsular lesions are seen. The previously noted mild hepatomegaly at presentation has improved slightly. The hepatic veins and inferior vena cava are patent.

GALLBLADDER:
The gallbladder is decompressed and not well visualized, consistent with indwelling biliary stenting and underlying biliary obstruction. No cholelithiasis or wall thickening is appreciated on the current study.

SPLEEN:
The spleen is normal in size and attenuation. No splenic lesions are identified. The splenic vein is patent without evidence of thrombosis or compression.

ADRENAL GLANDS:
The bilateral adrenal glands are within normal limits in size and morphology. No adrenal masses or nodules are identified.

KIDNEYS AND RETROPERITONEUM:
The kidneys are symmetric in size and demonstrate normal enhancement bilaterally. No hydronephrosis. No suspicious renal lesions. There is no retroperitoneal soft tissue mass or lymphadenopathy exceeding 1 cm in short axis. Small bilateral perirenal fat-stranding is noted, a nonspecific finding.

LYMPH NODES:
The previously noted peripancreatic lymph nodes have not meaningfully enlarged. The largest peripancreatic node now measures approximately 0.8 cm in short axis (previously up to 0.9 cm at baseline), and borderline nodes in the aortocaval and para-aortic regions remain stable and below threshold for pathologic enlargement. No new regional adenopathy is identified. No mesenteric or retrocrural adenopathy.

PERITONEUM AND ASCITES:
No ascites. No peritoneal thickening, nodularity, or implants to suggest peritoneal carcinomatosis. The omentum is unremarkable. No evidence of serosal implants along the bowel surfaces.

BOWEL:
The stomach, small bowel, and large bowel are unremarkable without evidence of wall thickening, obstruction, or pneumatosis. The duodenum demonstrates some mild compression at the C-loop in the region of the pancreatic head mass, unchanged from prior.

CHEST (LUNG BASES):
The lung bases are clear bilaterally without pleural effusion or consolidation. No pulmonary nodules are identified at the lung bases.

BONY STRUCTURES AND SOFT TISSUES:
No lytic or blastic osseous lesions are identified within the visualized axial skeleton. The visualized musculature and subcutaneous soft tissues show interval loss of soft tissue and fat volume compared with baseline imaging, consistent with the documented significant weight loss in this patient (approximately 20 pounds from initial presentation through the treatment course). No abdominal wall hernia.

IMPRESSION:

1. PANCREATIC HEAD MASS WITH CONTINUED TREATMENT RESPONSE: The hypoenhancing pancreatic head and uncinate process mass has further decreased in size to approximately 2.6 cm in greatest axial dimension, down from 3.0 cm at mid-treatment restaging and 3.8 cm at diagnosis, representing a cumulative reduction of approximately 32% from baseline following completion of 8 cycles of neoadjuvant chemotherapy. The overall appearance is consistent with ongoing treatment response.

2. SIGNIFICANTLY IMPROVED VASCULAR RELATIONSHIPS: The degree of soft tissue contact between the primary tumor and the superior mesenteric artery has substantially diminished, now estimated at less than 90 degrees of circumferential contact, a notable interval improvement from the approximately 120-degree abutment at mid-treatment restaging and 180-degree involvement at baseline. The superior mesenteric vein is now patent without evidence of significant narrowing, where previously focal luminal compromise was present. Taken together, these vascular findings suggest meaningful downstaging of the tumor's relationship to critical mesenteric vasculature following neoadjuvant treatment.

3. IMPROVED BILIARY AND PANCREATIC DUCTAL DILATION: Intrahepatic biliary dilation has substantially improved in the setting of the indwelling metallic biliary stent. Upstream main pancreatic duct dilation has also decreased, though mild dilation in the pancreatic body persists.

4. NO DISTANT METASTATIC DISEASE: No new hepatic lesions, peritoneal implants, or pathologically enlarged lymph nodes to suggest locoregional spread or distant metastatic disease.

5. PERIPANCREATIC LYMPH NODES: Previously described peripancreatic lymphadenopathy remains stable and sub-centimeter, with no convincing new nodal disease.

6. INTERVAL SOFT TISSUE VOLUME LOSS consistent with the patient's documented treatment-related weight loss.

Correlation with CA 19-9 level (reported as 85 U/mL at this timepoint, down from 310 U/mL at mid-treatment and 1,204 U/mL at diagnosis) is recommended for comprehensive clinical assessment. The findings on this examination are consistent with continued favorable treatment response to neoadjuvant chemotherapy, with improved vascular relationships relative to prior studies. Clinical and multidisciplinary reassessment is warranted to determine candidacy for consolidation chemoradiation and subsequent surgical consideration.

CHIEF COMPLAINT
Mrs. Margaret Osei-Bonsu is a 66-year-old woman of Ghanaian descent with borderline resectable pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (PDAC) of the pancreatic head and uncinate process who presents today following completion of 8 cycles of neoadjuvant chemotherapy (6 cycles modified FOLFIRINOX followed by 2 cycles FOLFIRI backbone due to oxaliplatin discontinuation for grade 3 neuropathy) for multidisciplinary tumor board review and planning of next treatment steps.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Mrs. Osei-Bonsu was initially diagnosed with borderline resectable PDAC approximately 9 months ago when she presented to her primary care physician with a 10-week history of painless progressive jaundice, 14-pound weight loss, new-onset epigastric discomfort radiating to the back, pale greasy stools, pruritus, and dark urine. Physical examination at that time revealed scleral icterus, mild hepatomegaly, and a positive Courvoisier sign. Initial labs showed a total bilirubin of 8.2 mg/dL, direct bilirubin 6.9 mg/dL, alkaline phosphatase 420 U/L, CA 19-9 1,204 U/mL, and CEA 7.1 ng/mL.

Pancreatic protocol CT and subsequent MRI/MRCP demonstrated a 3.8 cm hypoenhancing mass in the pancreatic head with upstream pancreatic duct dilation to 7 mm, common bile duct dilation to 16 mm, SMA abutment over approximately 170-180 degrees, and focal SMV narrowing without occlusion. No hepatic or peritoneal metastases were identified at baseline. Two sub-centimeter peripancreatic lymph nodes were noted. EUS-guided fine needle biopsy confirmed moderately differentiated pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (CK7+, CK19+, MUC1+, CK20-, CDX2-; Ki-67 ~35%). She was staged cT3 N1 M0, Stage IIB (AJCC 8th edition), borderline resectable by AHPBA/SSO/SSAT criteria.

She was discussed at the multidisciplinary pancreatic tumor board at diagnosis, and the decision was made to pursue neoadjuvant systemic therapy followed by reassessment for chemoradiation and surgery. Prior to chemotherapy initiation, she underwent uncomplicated ERCP with placement of a fully covered self-expanding metallic biliary stent (FCSEMS), with bilirubin declining to 1.8 mg/dL within two weeks.

She then initiated modified FOLFIRINOX: oxaliplatin 85 mg/m2, irinotecan 150 mg/m2 (dose-reduced from standard 180 mg/m2 at the outset given concern for biliary stent status and Gilbert syndrome), leucovorin 400 mg/m2, and 5-FU 2400 mg/m2 as 46-hour continuous infusion, every 14 days, with Neulasta (pegfilgrastim) prophylaxis each cycle.

During cycles 1-3, she developed grade 2 peripheral sensory neuropathy affecting the fingertips and toes, grade 2 fatigue, grade 1 nausea controlled with ondansetron and prochlorperazine, and one episode of grade 2 diarrhea managed with loperamide and a brief dose hold. CA 19-9 after cycle 2 had declined to 580 U/mL. A restaging CT after 4 cycles demonstrated the primary mass had decreased to 3.0 cm (from 3.8 cm), with SMA abutment reduced to approximately 120 degrees and improvement in SMV narrowing. CA 19-9 at that point was 310 U/mL. The decision was made to continue for a total of 8 cycles.

Unfortunately, during cycles 5-8, her peripheral neuropathy progressed from grade 2 to grade 3, becoming functionally limiting (difficulty buttoning clothing, unsteadiness on uneven surfaces), and oxaliplatin was discontinued after cycle 6. She completed cycles 7 and 8 on the FOLFIRI backbone (fluorouracil, leucovorin, irinotecan only). During this phase, she also lost an additional 6 pounds and experienced persistent grade 2 fatigue, though no hospitalizations were required.

A restaging CT obtained approximately 3 weeks ago, after completion of all 8 cycles, demonstrated the primary pancreatic head mass measuring 2.6 cm (down from 3.8 cm at baseline, representing a 32% reduction in maximum diameter), with SMA abutment estimated at less than 90 degrees. The SMV appeared patent without significant narrowing. No evidence of hepatic or peritoneal metastases was identified. CA 19-9 at completion of chemotherapy was 85 U/mL, representing a 93% decline from the pre-treatment peak of 1,204 U/mL.

This case was reviewed in today's multidisciplinary pancreatic tumor board. Present at the tumor board were surgical oncology (hepatobiliary), radiation oncology, interventional radiology, diagnostic radiology, pathology, and medical oncology. The imaging, tumor biology, pathologic confirmation, and the patient's current clinical status were reviewed in detail. The board discussion and consensus recommendation are summarized under Assessment/Plan below.

The patient has been accompanied today by her daughter. She is overall doing better compared to her status mid-chemotherapy. She has noted some recovery in energy. ASSESSMENT

1. Borderline resectable pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (PDAC), pancreatic head and uncinate process — favorable response to neoadjuvant modified FOLFIRINOX/FOLFIRI.

Mrs. Osei-Bonsu has had a meaningful response to 8 cycles of neoadjuvant chemotherapy. Her CA 19-9 has declined 93% from a pre-treatment peak of 1,204 U/mL to 85 U/mL. Cross-sectional imaging demonstrates a 32% reduction in maximum tumor diameter (3.8 → 2.6 cm) and a substantial reduction in SMA abutment from approximately 170-180 degrees to under 90 degrees, with restoration of SMV patency. This degree of radiographic and serologic response is highly encouraging in the setting of borderline resectable disease. There is no evidence of distant metastatic disease on the most recent restaging CT. She has no evidence of disease progression.

2. Chemotherapy-induced peripheral neuropathy (CIPN) — improving on treatment break, now approximately grade 1-2.

Her neuropathy was the primary driver of oxaliplatin discontinuation after cycle 6. With approximately 4 weeks off all neurotoxic chemotherapy, she has recovered to an estimated grade 1-2 level. Ongoing monitoring is important, as she may receive additional neurotoxic agents (capecitabine as a radiosensitizer has modest neurotoxicity).

3. Type 2 diabetes mellitus — worsening glycemic control in the setting of cancer-related stress, chemotherapy effect, and possibly early pancreatic exocrine/endocrine dysfunction.

HbA1c 7.4%, fasting blood glucose trending in the 115-160 range. She is currently diet-controlled only. Will communicate findings to her PCP with a recommendation for close glucose monitoring, particularly in anticipation of chemoradiation which may further affect appetite, nutrition, and glucose control. If glucose control worsens, pharmacologic intervention (metformin or basal insulin) will be needed, especially ahead of a potential Whipple procedure which carries known risk of new-onset or worsened diabetes.

4. Mild anemia — stable, likely multifactorial (chemotherapy-related myelosuppression, nutritional, possible chronic disease).

Hemoglobin 10.4 g/dL. Improving from nadir. No transfusion requirement at this time. Iron studies and B12/folate to be checked if not already done recently.

5. Nutritional status — ongoing concern; albumin mildly depressed at 3.2 g/dL.

Patient has lost approximately 22 lbs total from pre-diagnosis weight. Albumin 3.2 g/dL. She will be referred to our pancreatic oncology dietitian for intensive nutritional optimization during the window between completing chemotherapy and initiation of chemoradiation, and in the peri-surgical period.

PLAN

Multidisciplinary Tumor Board Consensus — Today's Discussion and Recommendation:

The case was reviewed in the multidisciplinary pancreatic tumor board today. All available cross-sectional imaging (baseline CT, MRI/MRCP, restaging CTs after 4 cycles and after 8 cycles) was presented by our abdominal radiologist. The pathology (original EUS biopsy — moderately differentiated PDAC, CK7+/CK19+/MUC1+) was reviewed. The clinical trajectory (CA 19-9 from 1,204 → 85 U/mL; tumor reduction from 3.8 cm → 2.6 cm; SMA abutment from ~175° → <90°; SMV now patent) was summarized. The patient's current performance status (ECOG 1), neuropathy recovery, nutritional status, and comorbidities (diabetes, hypertension) were reviewed.

Board consensus recommendation:

1. Consolidation chemoradiation prior to surgical resection:
   The board unanimously recommended consolidation chemoradiation as the next treatment step, with the goals of further sterilizing the surgical margin (particularly the retroperitoneal/SMA margin, which remains of concern given the history of abutment), addressing potential micrometastatic residual disease in the surgical field, and maximizing the likelihood of an R0 resection. The addition of pre-operative chemoradiation in this setting is supported by several institutional experiences and trial data, including the role of radiation in improving locoregional control in borderline resectable disease. Given the degree of response to chemotherapy and the restored SMV patency, the board felt the patient now has a reasonable chance at an R0 Whipple.

   Radiation oncology is planning:
   - IMRT (intensity-modulated radiation therapy) with CBCT-based daily image guidance
   - Treatment to the primary tumor with appropriate margin, with coverage of the celiac axis nodal region and peripancreatic lymph nodes
   - Concurrent capecitabine (Xeloda) as a radiosensitizer at standard dosing (825 mg/m2 twice daily on radiation days)
   - Radiation oncology will perform simulation and planning in the next 1-2 weeks; treatment to begin approximately 3 weeks from today

   The patient will be seen by radiation oncology for a formal consultation (see separate note when available).

2. Reassessment of surgical candidacy approximately 4-6 weeks after completion of chemoradiation:
   Following chemoradiation, repeat pancreatic protocol CT will be obtained to assess for response and confirm absence of distant metastatic disease before proceeding to surgery. Hepatobiliary surgery has reviewed the case and agrees to plan for open pylorus-preserving pancreaticoduodenectomy (Whipple procedure) pending continued radiographic eligibility at that time. They noted the SMV involvement will likely require careful dissection and possible venous resection/reconstruction, and the family has been informed of this possibility.

3. Monitoring and supportive care in the interim period:
   - Nutritional optimization: Referral to pancreatic oncology dietitian. High-calorie, high-protein diet with possible oral nutritional supplements. Target weight stabilization prior to chemoradiation. Patient counseled to aim for 25-30 kcal/kg/day. Pancreatic enzymes may be considered if exocrine insufficiency symptoms develop (pale greasy stools, bloating).
   - Neuropathy: Continue to monitor. If grade worsens during chemoradiation (capecitabine has modest potential), dose modification of capecitabine may be needed. Duloxetine 30 mg daily offered to patient for symptomatic relief of neuropathic discomfort; patient agreed to a trial.
   - Glucose monitoring: Communicate to PCP that HbA1c is 7.4% and fasting glucoses are trending upward. Recommend close monitoring and initiation of pharmacologic therapy if fasting glucose consistently exceeds 180 mg/dL, particularly in anticipation of peri-operative period. Patient referred back to PCP for diabetes management optimization prior to surgery.
   - Anemia: Recheck CBC in 3 weeks (prior to chemoradiation simulation visit). Obtain iron studies and B12/folate. If hemoglobin drops below 9.0 g/dL or she becomes symptomatic, may consider IV iron or erythropoiesis-stimulating agent cautiously given oncologic context.
   - CA 19-9 and CEA: Will recheck at radiation oncology simulation visit and again prior to surgery to track trajectory.
   - Hypertension: Continue amlodipine. Blood pressure well controlled today.

4. Genomic testing and germline counseling:
   Germline BRCA1/2 testing has been recommended given the importance of identifying hereditary risk; this is pending or has been ordered — to be confirmed with patient and primary care. Somatic NGS on the diagnostic biopsy specimen has been ordered and results are expected. These results will be important for understanding tumor biology and potential therapeutic vulnerability (e.g., PARP inhibitor eligibility for BRCA-mutant disease, PD-L1 status for immunotherapy context).

5. Goals of care and psychosocial support:

CT ABDOMEN AND PELVIS WITH PANCREATIC PROTOCOL (TRIPLE-PHASE CONTRAST) AND PET-CT WHOLE BODY
Pre-Surgical Restaging Examination

CLINICAL INDICATION:
66-year-old female with biopsy-proven pancreatic head ductal adenocarcinoma (moderately differentiated), initially staged as borderline resectable with superior mesenteric artery (SMA) abutment and superior mesenteric vein (SMV) involvement. Patient completed 8 cycles of neoadjuvant chemotherapy (modified FOLFIRINOX x 6 cycles followed by FOLFIRI x 2 cycles) with documented partial response on prior restaging CT. Subsequently completed long-course chemoradiation (IMRT, 54 Gy in 28 fractions with concurrent capecitabine as radiosensitizer), concluding approximately 5 weeks prior to this examination. Biliary stent in situ. Current CA 19-9: 52 U/mL. This study was ordered for pre-operative surgical planning and assessment of resectability.

--- CT ABDOMEN AND PELVIS WITH PANCREATIC PROTOCOL ---

TECHNIQUE:
Multiphase CT of the abdomen and pelvis was performed on a 64-slice MDCT scanner following administration of 120 mL of intravenous iodinated contrast (Omnipaque 350). Imaging was acquired in the non-contrast, early arterial, late arterial (pancreatic parenchymal), and portal venous phases at 0.625 mm collimation with coronal and sagittal multiplanar reformations. Oral contrast was not administered. Pancreatic protocol technique was used with bolus tracking at the aortic level. Images were reviewed at standard soft tissue, liver, and lung windows.

FINDINGS:

Pancreas and Primary Tumor:
In the region of the pancreatic head and uncinate process, there is a persistent area of hypoenhancement relative to the adjacent pancreatic parenchyma, now measuring approximately 2.3 cm in greatest axial dimension (previously 2.6 cm on the prior CT performed following completion of neoadjuvant chemotherapy, and 3.8 cm at baseline staging). The lesion demonstrates continued hypoenhancement across all phases with surrounding desmoplastic soft tissue reaction, which is expected following chemoradiation and is consistent with residual/treated primary tumor. The margins of the lesion are somewhat indistinct, which in the post-treatment setting reflects a combination of treatment effect, fibrosis, and possibly residual viable tumor — this is a characteristic appearance following neoadjuvant chemoradiation in pancreatic adenocarcinoma and does not necessarily indicate disease progression. No new areas of suspicious hypoenhancement within the gland are seen.

The upstream main pancreatic duct in the neck and body measures approximately 4.5 mm in caliber, representing improvement compared to the 7 mm dilation seen on the initial staging CT, consistent with partial decompression of the obstructing mass. A focal area of pancreatic parenchymal atrophy in the body and tail distal to the mass is noted, unchanged from prior and attributed to chronic ductal obstruction.

The peripancreatic soft tissue stranding in the region of the uncinate process is extensive and may represent a combination of desmoplastic reaction, post-radiation fibrosis, and residual disease; this finding is not meaningfully distinguishable from the prior post-chemotherapy restaging examination.

Vascular Relationships:
Superior Mesenteric Artery (SMA): The previously documented abutment of the primary mass against the SMA has continued to diminish with multimodality treatment. The soft tissue contact along the right lateral wall of the SMA is now estimated at less than 60 degrees of circumferential involvement (reduced from approximately 120 degrees following neoadjuvant chemotherapy, and approximately 180 degrees at baseline). There is no convincing evidence of arterial encasement, irregularity of the arterial wall, or loss of the fat plane along the majority of the SMA circumference. The SMA origin and proximal course are patent with no hemodynamically significant narrowing. This represents a meaningful improvement in the vascular interface and is consistent with an evolving response to multimodality therapy.

Superior Mesenteric Vein (SMV) and Portal Vein: The SMV is now patent throughout its course, without evidence of focal narrowing or luminal compromise at the level of the pancreatic head mass. The previously described focal venous narrowing at the interface with the mass has resolved on the current examination. The portal confluence and main portal vein are widely patent. No venous thrombosis is identified.

Celiac Axis and Common Hepatic Artery: No abutment or involvement. The celiac axis and its major branches, including the proper hepatic artery, left gastric artery, and splenic artery, appear normal in course and caliber without peri-arterial soft tissue thickening.

Splenic Vein: Patent. No evidence of thrombosis.

Biliary System:
A fully covered self-expanding metallic biliary stent (FCSEMS) is in place within the common bile duct, with the proximal end in the hepatic duct confluence and distal end extending into the duodenum. The stent is patent with appropriate luminal opacification. The intrahepatic bile ducts are not dilated. The gallbladder is absent (post-ERCP / stent placement). There is no new biliary obstruction or evidence of cholangitis.

Liver:
The liver is normal in size. No focal hepatic lesions are identified. No hypodense or rim-enhancing lesions suspicious for metastatic disease. The hepatic parenchyma enhances homogeneously. There is no biliary dilation. No perihepatic fluid.

Lymph Nodes:
In the peripancreatic region, two small soft tissue density lymph nodes are again identified, the largest measuring approximately 0.7 cm in short axis (previously up to 0.9 cm at baseline). These nodes have decreased in size compared to initial staging and are not enlarged by size criteria on the current examination. No new pathologically enlarged lymph nodes are identified in the celiac, para-aortic, portacaval, or mesenteric distributions. No mediastinal or hilar adenopathy (assessed on lung windows).

Spleen:
Unremarkable. Normal size and attenuation. No splenic lesions.

Adrenal Glands:
Bilaterally unremarkable. No adrenal nodules or masses.

Kidneys:
Both kidneys are normal in size and demonstrate symmetric parenchymal enhancement. No hydronephrosis. No renal masses. A 3 mm hypodense focus in the right renal cortex is too small to characterize but is statistically most likely a tiny cyst; unchanged from prior study.

Stomach and Bowel:
The stomach and proximal duodenum appear somewhat compressed by the mass effect from the region of the pancreatic head. The duodenum demonstrates mild wall thickening in the second portion adjacent to the mass, which may be secondary to post-radiation inflammatory change. The remainder of the small and large bowel is unremarkable without obstruction or perforation. No bowel wall thickening or pneumatosis.

Peritoneum and Retroperitoneum:
No ascites. No peritoneal soft tissue nodularity or implants. No omental thickening or cake formation. The retroperitoneum is unremarkable without bulky soft tissue masses.

Chest Bases (included on CT):
Small bilateral pleural effusions are not present. No lung nodules at the lung bases bilaterally. No consolidation.

Miscellaneous:
Surgical biliary stent in situ as described. No other surgical hardware. No pneumoperitoneum.

--- PET-CT (18F-FDG WHOLE BODY) ---

TECHNIQUE:
Whole body FDG-PET/CT was performed following intravenous administration of approximately 12.4 mCi of 18F-fluorodeoxyglucose (FDG). The patient fasted for a minimum of 6 hours prior to injection. Serum glucose at time of injection: 118 mg/dL (mildly elevated in the setting of known type 2 diabetes; acceptable for study interpretation). Imaging was performed from the skull base to mid-thighs at 90 minutes post-injection. Low-dose CT was used for attenuation correction and anatomical co-registration.

FINDINGS:

Primary Pancreatic Mass:
Moderately increased FDG avidity is identified in the region of the pancreatic head and uncinate process, co-localizing with the hypoenhancing mass on the concurrent CT. The maximum standardized uptake value (SUVmax) measures approximately 4.1. This is notably reduced compared to the SUVmax obtained on prior functional imaging at initial staging, in keeping with metabolic response to multimodality therapy. The area of residual FDG uptake is consistent with viable residual tumor in the context of ongoing treatment effect and fibrosis; however, treatment-related metabolic activity cannot be entirely excluded and is a recognized limitation of post-radiation FDG-PET interpretation. The overall pattern is most consistent with a partial metabolic response.

Liver:
No FDG-avid hepatic lesions. The liver background is physiologically homogeneous. No focal areas of abnormal tracer accumulation.

Lymph Nodes:
The peripancreatic lymph nodes, as described on CT, do not demonstrate FDG avidity above background. No pathologically FDG-avid lymph nodes in the mesenteric, celiac, retroperitoneal, or pelvic nodal stations. No mediastinal or hilar FDG-avid adenopathy.

Lung:
No FDG-avid pulmonary nodules or masses. No pleural-based FDG avidity. The lung parenchyma appears clear without consolidation.

Bone:
No FDG-avid osseous lesions suspicious for skeletal metastatic disease. The axial and appendicular skeleton demonstrates normal background marrow activity. No focal lytic or sclerotic lesions on the concurrent CT to suggest bone involvement.

Pelvis:
No pelvic FDG-avid masses. The uterus and adnexa are not separately delineated; no definitive pelvic lesion.

Peritoneum:
No FDG-avid peritoneal implants. The peritoneal surfaces and omentum are metabolically quiescent.

Other:
Physiologic FDG activity in the brain, myocardium, liver, spleen, kidneys, ureters, and bladder. Biliary stent in situ without associated hypermetabolism. No unexpected sites of abnormal tracer accumulation.

IMPRESSION:

1. Primary pancreatic head / uncinate process mass: Further decrease in size since completion of neoadjuvant chemotherapy, now measuring approximately 2.3 cm (down from 2.6 cm post-chemotherapy and 3.8 cm at initial staging), representing a cumulative reduction of approximately 39% from baseline in response to multimodality neoadjuvant therapy. The degree of SMA contact continues to diminish and is now estimated at less than 60 degrees of circumferential abutment, without evidence of arterial encasement or wall irregularity. The SMV is patent throughout with resolution of the previously observed focal narrowing.

2. No hepatic metastases, peritoneal implants, or distant FDG-avid disease. No new suspicious lymphadenopathy.

3. Residual moderate FDG avidity in the primary mass (SUVmax 4.1) is decreased from prior staging, consistent with partial metabolic response; viable residual tumor and/or post-radiation inflammatory change are both contributory and cannot be reliably distinguished at this interval.

4. Patent biliary stent. No biliary obstruction.

5. Overall, the imaging findings are favorable for surgical planning. Vascular anatomy as described above should be reviewed carefully by the hepatobiliary surgical team in the context of operative planning, with specific attention to the SMA-tumor interface and the previously involved SMV segment.

MICROSCOPIC DESCRIPTION:

Sections of the pancreatic head tumor show a moderately differentiated ductal adenocarcinoma with extensive treatment effect. The tumor is characterized by infiltrative glands and tubular structures, often with angulated, irregular contours set within an extensively desmoplastic and hyalinized stroma. The cancer cells display moderate nuclear pleomorphism, prominent nucleoli, and a moderate amount of pale eosinophilic cytoplasm. Mitotic figures are infrequent in the post-treatment specimen (1-2 per 10 high-power fields). Large areas of the original tumor bed show acellular hyalinized fibrosis, dystrophic calcification, and small foci of foamy macrophage aggregates, consistent with necrosis and treatment-induced fibrosis. Acellular mucin pools surrounded by fibrotic stroma are present without viable mucin-producing tumor cells; these are scored as necrotic/treatment effect per CAP regression grading criteria.

Residual viable tumor nests are present, predominantly at the periphery of the fibrotic tumor bed and within perineural spaces. The viable tumor component represents less than 50% of the original tumor bed area but is more than isolated single cells or rare small clusters, consistent with CAP Tumor Regression Grade 2 (moderate response).

Perineural invasion is identified in multiple sections, involving large-caliber autonomic nerve bundles within the peripancreatic soft tissue of the uncinate process. This is a recognized adverse pathologic feature in PDAC and is noted for prognostic purposes.

Lymphovascular invasion is not identified on routine H&E staining after examination of multiple levels; IHC for D2-40 (podoplanin) and CD31 was not performed as lymphovascular invasion was not suspected on H&E review.

The one positive lymph node (1/22) contains a 0.4 cm focus of metastatic moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma with a central area of fibrosis, also suggesting treatment effect within the nodal deposit. Extranodal extension is not identified.

All other surgical margins are free of invasive carcinoma as described above.

Pylorus-preserving pancreaticoduodenectomy with en bloc SMV resection:
MODERATELY DIFFERENTIATED PANCREATIC DUCTAL ADENOCARCINOMA, 2.1 cm, WITH EXTENSIVE TREATMENT EFFECT (CAP TUMOR REGRESSION GRADE 2), PANCREATIC HEAD AND UNCINATE PROCESS.
Perineural invasion present. Lymphovascular invasion absent.
Margins: ALL NEGATIVE (R0). Closest margin: SMA/retroperitoneal margin, 1.2 mm.
Lymph nodes: 1/22 positive for metastatic adenocarcinoma.
