HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Ms. Margaret Osei-Bonsu is a 67-year-old woman of Ghanaian descent with a history of borderline resectable pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (PDAC) of the pancreatic head and uncinate process, initially diagnosed approximately 18 months ago, who now presents with radiographically and pathologically confirmed recurrence in the form of a solitary hepatic metastasis.

By way of background, her initial presentation was notable for progressive painless obstructive jaundice, an 14-pound weight loss, and epigastric pain radiating to the back. Workup revealed a 3.8 cm hypoenhancing pancreatic head mass abutting the SMA (approximately 180 degrees) with SMV involvement and upstream biliary and pancreatic ductal dilation. CA 19-9 at diagnosis was 1,204 U/mL. EUS-guided biopsy confirmed moderately differentiated pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (CK7+, CK19+, MUC1+, Ki-67 ~35%). She was staged as clinical T3 N1 M0, borderline resectable.

She received a biliary stent (FCSEMS) via ERCP, after which her bilirubin normalized from 8.2 to 1.8 mg/dL, and she then initiated modified FOLFIRINOX (oxaliplatin 85 mg/m2, irinotecan 150 mg/m2, leucovorin 400 mg/m2, 5-FU 2400 mg/m2 over 46 hours, q14 days) as neoadjuvant chemotherapy. She received 6 cycles of FOLFIRINOX before oxaliplatin was discontinued due to grade 3 peripheral sensory neuropathy (interfering with ADLs); she then completed 2 additional cycles with FOLFIRI (fluorouracil, leucovorin, irinotecan) for a total of 8 neoadjuvant cycles. Over this period her CA 19-9 declined from 1,204 to 85 U/mL, and restaging CT showed the primary tumor had decreased to 2.6 cm with SMA abutment reduced to less than 90 degrees and restoration of SMV patency, consistent with a partial treatment response.

Following the multidisciplinary tumor board's recommendation, she underwent consolidation chemoradiation (54 Gy in 28 fractions by IMRT with simultaneous integrated boost; concurrent capecitabine 825 mg/m2 BID on radiation days). Pre-operative CT and PET-CT showed further reduction of the primary mass to 2.3 cm, SMA contact less than 60 degrees, no FDG-avid distant disease (SUVmax 4.1), and CA 19-9 of 52 U/mL.

She subsequently underwent open pylorus-preserving pancreaticoduodenectomy (Whipple procedure) with en bloc segmental SMV resection and end-to-end venous reconstruction. Final surgical pathology demonstrated moderately differentiated PDAC, 2.1 cm, ypT3 N1 M0 (1/22 positive lymph nodes), with perineural invasion present, lymphovascular invasion absent, CAP tumor regression grade 2 (moderate response), and an R0 resection with the tightest margin being the SMA/retroperitoneal margin at 1.2 mm. Comprehensive NGS on the resection specimen revealed KRAS p.G12V (VAF 42%), TP53 p.R175H (VAF 38%), homozygous SMAD4 deletion, and CDKN2A/p16 deletion — a high-risk genomic profile — with MSS, low TMB (3 mut/Mb), no BRCA1/2, ATM, or PALB2 pathogenic mutations, and no fusions detected.

The postoperative course was complicated by an ISGPF grade B pancreatic fistula managed conservatively with prolonged drain maintenance, TPN, and octreotide, with eventual drain removal at 6 weeks. She was then initiated on adjuvant gemcitabine/capecitabine (GemCap) per ESPAC-4 data, with gemcitabine 1000 mg/m2 days 1, 8, 15 of a 28-day cycle and capecitabine started at 80% dose (1330 mg/m2/day, days 1-21). Adjuvant therapy was complicated by a minor recurrent pancreatic fistula (grade A, managed conservatively), grade 2 hand-foot syndrome necessitating capecitabine dose reduction to 75%, grade 2 cytopenias requiring gemcitabine dose reduction to 750 mg/m2 and day-15 omissions in multiple cycles, and progressive peripheral neuropathy to grade 2 on background of oxaliplatin-related neuropathy. She completed all 6 planned cycles of adjuvant GemCap at reduced doses. CA 19-9 at end of adjuvant treatment was 19 U/mL.

Post-treatment surveillance CT approximately 8 months after surgery showed no evidence of recurrent or metastatic disease. At routine surveillance approximately 14 months after the Whipple procedure, the patient was doing well — CA 19-9 24 U/mL, CEA 2.2 ng/mL — and had resumed volunteer work at the local library.

At approximately 17 months post-Whipple, routine surveillance CT abdomen/pelvis identified a new 1.4 cm hypodense lesion in hepatic segment VI. CA 19-9 had risen to 89 U/mL (from a nadir of 18 U/mL) and CEA had risen to 4.8 ng/mL. Urgent MRI liver with gadoxetate disodium (Eovist) was performed and showed the segment VI lesion to have hepatobiliary phase hypointensity, restricted diffusion, and arterial rim enhancement with washout — features diagnostic of metastatic disease. No additional hepatic lesions were identified and concurrent CT chest showed no extrahepatic disease.

CT-guided percutaneous core needle biopsy of the hepatic lesion confirmed metastatic adenocarcinoma of pancreatobiliary origin (CK7+, CK19+, MUC1+, CA 19-9+, CK20-, CDX2-, TTF-1-). Repeat NGS on the metastatic sample confirmed an identical driver mutation profile to the primary: KRAS p.G12V, TP53 p.R175H, SMAD4 loss. No new actionable alterations were identified. Microsatellite stable confirmed. BRCA1/2 somatic mutations absent.

The case was reviewed today at the multidisciplinary pancreatic tumor board, attended by hepatobiliary surgery, radiation oncology, interventional radiology, medical oncology, and palliative care.

From the patient's perspective, she reports feeling well at baseline since completing adjuvant therapy. Energy has been good. She has maintained her weight and is eating adequately. She continues pancrelipase (Creon) with meals for exocrine pancreatic insufficiency and notes that her stool consistency and absorption have been acceptable. Her type 2 diabetes (worsened post-pancreatectomy) is managed with metformin and basal insulin glargine; she reports adequate glucose control under endocrinology co-management. She continues to have grade 1 peripheral neuropathy in her fingertips, which she describes as mild numbness, stable and not progressing since completing adjuvant GemCap. She denies new abdominal pain, jaundice, weight loss, or constitutional symptoms. She is appropriately anxious about the new diagnosis but expressed a clear desire to understand her options and to continue fighting. She asks specifically about whether the liver lesion can be removed.

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

Ms. Osei-Bonsu is a 67-year-old woman with KRAS G12V-mutant, TP53-mutant, SMAD4-deleted, MSS pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma, originally borderline resectable, who was treated with a multimodal curative-intent approach comprising biliary decompression, neoadjuvant modified FOLFIRINOX (8 cycles with oxaliplatin discontinued after cycle 6 for grade 3 neuropathy, completed as FOLFIRI), consolidation chemoradiation (54 Gy IMRT with concurrent capecitabine), and ultimately R0 pylorus-preserving pancreaticoduodenectomy with SMV resection. She completed adjuvant GemCap (6 cycles at reduced doses due to hematologic toxicity, hand-foot syndrome, and neuropathy). She now presents with pathologically confirmed solitary hepatic metastasis (segment VI, 1.4 cm) identified at 17 months post-resection, representing disease recurrence in the metastatic setting.

This case was presented in full at today's multidisciplinary pancreatic tumor board. The key questions deliberated were: (1) Can this oligometastatic disease be addressed with locoregional therapy — surgical hepatic resection, ablation, or stereotactic body radiation — either alone or in combination with systemic therapy? (2) What is the optimal systemic regimen given her prior treatment exposure? (3) What is the appropriate framing of goals of care?

Regarding locoregional therapy: Hepatobiliary surgery was consulted. While technically a solitary 1.4 cm hepatic metastasis in segment VI could be anatomically resectable, the board noted that in the context of pancreatic cancer with its high propensity for micrometastatic dissemination, the clinical benefit of upfront resection in this setting is not established, and there is significant concern that proceeding directly to hepatectomy without systemic therapy would expose the patient to surgical risk without controlling presumed occult microscopic disease. The prior history of abdominal surgery (Whipple with SMV reconstruction) increases the technical complexity and perioperative risk of any hepatic resection. Interventional radiology noted that image-guided thermal ablation (microwave or radiofrequency ablation) could be considered for a lesion of this size and location, though again the board consensus was that systemic therapy should come first. Radiation oncology noted that hepatic SBRT to the segment VI lesion is feasible and could be revisited in the oligometastatic consolidation setting after documentation of systemic disease control. The board decision was unanimous: initiate systemic chemotherapy first, reassess after 2-3 cycles for evidence of systemic disease control and oligometastatic stability, and then discuss locoregional therapy for hepatic consolidation if there is a good response without new metastatic sites.

Regarding systemic therapy: The patient has received, in order: modified FOLFIRINOX (6 cycles) → FOLFIRI (2 cycles, after oxaliplatin discontinuation), then chemoradiation with capecitabine, then adjuvant gemcitabine plus capecitabine (6 cycles). In the metastatic setting, the two best-established first-line regimens for PDAC — FOLFIRINOX/mFOLFIRINOX and gemcitabine/nab-paclitaxel — must be considered against her prior exposure. Her exposure to fluorouracil, leucovorin, and irinotecan in the neoadjuvant setting argues against re-challenging with FOLFIRINOX as optimal first-line palliative therapy, particularly given persistent peripheral neuropathy that would worsen with oxaliplatin rechallenge. Her adjuvant GemCap already included gemcitabine and capecitabine. There is reasonable evidence, however, that gemcitabine/nab-paclitaxel (Abraxane) remains an active and appropriate regimen even in patients who have received prior gemcitabine-based therapy in the adjuvant setting, particularly given the disease-free interval of 17 months and the established systemic activity of nab-paclitaxel in PDAC. The nab-paclitaxel component provides meaningful additional activity distinct from capecitabine, and the disease-free interval of more than one year makes rechallenge with gemcitabine-containing therapy biologically reasonable.

The NAPOLI-3 trial data (NALIRIFOX: nanoliposomal irinotecan, oxaliplatin, 5-FU/leucovorin) in the first-line metastatic setting was discussed; however, given her prior extensive exposure to both oxaliplatin and irinotecan, and grade 3 oxaliplatin-induced neuropathy in the prior setting, this regimen is not felt to be appropriate.

No actionable genomic alterations were identified on NGS of either the primary or the metastatic biopsy: BRCA1/2 wild-type (confirmed on both somatic and germline testing), ATM and PALB2 wild-type, MSS with low TMB (3 mut/Mb) — precluding benefit from olaparib maintenance, atezolizumab, or pembrolizumab immune checkpoint inhibition. KRAS G12V is not currently targetable with approved agents (KRAS G12C inhibitors such as sotorasib and adagrasib do not cover the G12V variant). HER2 amplification absent. The KRAS G12V mutation does, however, remain a potentially relevant target for emerging investigational agents, and clinical trial enrollment should be explored.

Plan:

1. Initiate gemcitabine/nab-paclitaxel for metastatic PDAC. Gemcitabine 1000 mg/m2 IV and nab-paclitaxel (Abraxane) 125 mg/m2 IV on days 1, 8, and 15 of each 28-day cycle. Treatment intent is palliative/disease control, with the primary endpoint being systemic disease control allowing consideration of hepatic locoregional consolidation.

2. Monitor CBC, CMP, and CA 19-9 / CEA at the start of each cycle. Given prior grade 2 cytopenias on adjuvant GemCap, will monitor closely for myelosuppression; G-CSF (filgrastim) will be available as rescue but not initiated prophylactically at this time given current blood counts.

3. Monitor peripheral neuropathy closely given cumulative exposure to oxaliplatin (prior grade 3) and now introduction of nab-paclitaxel, which carries its own significant risk of peripheral neuropathy. Patient counseled that she may experience worsening numbness and/or tingling in hands and feet with nab-paclitaxel; nab-paclitaxel dose reduction or discontinuation will be considered if neuropathy worsens to grade 2 or above.

4. Restaging CT chest, abdomen, and pelvis after 2 cycles (approximately 8 weeks) to assess systemic disease response and determine eligibility for hepatic locoregional consolidation.

5. Clinical trial: Patient will be referred to the oncology clinical trials team for evaluation of eligibility for trials targeting KRAS G12V or other relevant targets in PDAC. Additionally, any available trials for second-line or subsequent therapy in MSS, BRCA-wild-type PDAC will be noted for future reference.

6. Nutrition: Continue pancrelipase (Creon) with meals. Nutrition team consultation to be arranged given ongoing importance of nutritional support during palliative systemic therapy. Albumin 3.6 g/dL — acceptable for initiation of therapy.

7. Diabetes: Continue current regimen per endocrinology. Alert endocrinology to new chemotherapy initiation; nausea and appetite changes may affect glucose management.

8. Goals of care: A full goals of care conversation was conducted today with the patient. She understands that her cancer has returned in the liver and that the goal of treatment at this stage is disease control rather than cure. She expressed her wish to pursue active treatment and to maintain quality of life. She has a durable power of attorney for healthcare designated. Palliative care team was introduced today and will co-follow during palliative chemotherapy for symptom management and ongoing goals-of-care discussions.

9. Follow-up: Patient will be seen in clinic in 2 weeks prior to cycle 1 day 8 for toxicity assessment and labs. She will be seen at the start of cycle 2 (day 29) for full assessment.

Time spent with patient: approximately 75 minutes, including multidisciplinary tumor board review time. Questions answered thoroughly. Patient verbalized understanding of the diagnosis, treatment plan, and next steps.

MICROSCOPIC DESCRIPTION:

Histologic sections of the core needle biopsy demonstrate fibrous and desmoplastic stromal tissue infiltrated by atypical epithelial elements. The neoplastic cells are arranged in irregularly shaped glandular structures, cribriform nests, and occasional single infiltrative cells dispersed within a densely fibrotic, hypocellular desmoplastic stroma. Individual tumor cells exhibit enlarged, pleomorphic, hyperchromatic nuclei with prominent nucleoli and moderate amounts of pale eosinophilic to amphophilic cytoplasm. Nuclear-to-cytoplasmic ratios are increased. Luminal necrosis (so-called "dirty necrosis") is focally identified within some glandular lumina. Nuclear contour irregularity and occasional mitotic figures (approximately 4 per 10 high-power fields) are present. The overall architecture is consistent with moderately differentiated (grade 2) carcinoma.

The surrounding stroma is markedly desmoplastic with dense collagenous fibrosis and embedded spindle-shaped fibroblasts and myofibroblasts, a hallmark of pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma. Scattered chronic inflammatory cells, predominantly lymphocytes, are present within the stroma. No granulomas are identified. No normal pancreatic acinar parenchyma is observed within the biopsy cores, suggesting complete replacement by tumor and reactive stroma at the sampled site.

No lymphovascular invasion is identified within the biopsy material (note: assessment limited by the nature of the specimen). No perineural invasion is identified in the sampled cores.

Cell block sections show similar atypical glandular clusters with the morphologic features described above. Background material includes blood and proteinaceous debris.

COMMENT:
The histologic and immunophenotypic features of this EUS-guided fine needle biopsy are diagnostic of moderately differentiated pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma arising from the pancreatic head. The desmoplastic stromal reaction with infiltrative irregular glands and nuclear atypia, combined with the supporting IHC profile (CK7+, CK19+, MUC1+, CA 19-9+, CK20-, CDX2-, synaptophysin-, chromogranin-, trypsin-), is characteristic of conventional pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma. Loss of SMAD4/DPC4 expression by IHC is identified in approximately 55% of pancreatic ductal adenocarcinomas and is an adverse prognostic finding. Aberrant p53 overexpression is consistent with a TP53 missense gain-of-function mutation (most commonly R175H or R248W/Q), which is identified in approximately 70% of PDAC cases. Confirmatory molecular studies including MSI assessment and comprehensive somatic NGS are pending and will be reported as addenda.

The clinical and radiographic context (3.8 cm hypoenhancing pancreatic head mass with biliary and pancreatic ductal obstruction, markedly elevated CA 19-9, and imaging features of borderline resectable disease) is concordant with this histologic diagnosis.

She described a 10-week progressive history of painless jaundice, associated with significant pruritus, dark urine, and pale, greasy stools consistent with obstructive jaundice and exocrine insufficiency. She also endorsed approximately 14 pounds of unintentional weight loss over this period, which she attributed initially to reduced appetite related to epigastric discomfort. She described a dull, gnawing epigastric pain that radiates into the mid-back, worse after meals. She denies any prior history of pancreatitis, pancreatic cysts, or prior abdominal malignancy.

On examination at the time of initial presentation, she was noted to have scleral icterus, mild hepatomegaly, and a palpable, non-tender gallbladder (a Courvoisier sign), raising immediate suspicion for malignant biliary obstruction from a pancreatic head lesion. Laboratory evaluation was notable for total bilirubin 8.2 mg/dL (direct 6.9 mg/dL), alkaline phosphatase 420 U/L, AST 98 U/L, ALT 112 U/L, albumin 3.1 g/dL, CA 19-9 markedly elevated at 1,204 U/mL, and CEA mildly elevated at 7.1 ng/mL.

Subsequent pancreatic protocol CT demonstrated a 3.8 cm hypoenhancing mass in the pancreatic head and uncinate process with upstream main pancreatic duct dilation to 7 mm and common bile duct dilation to 16 mm. The mass was found to abut the superior mesenteric artery (SMA) over approximately 180 degrees of its circumference and involved the superior mesenteric vein (SMV) with focal luminal narrowing but without complete occlusion. No hepatic lesions or peritoneal implants were identified. Two sub-centimeter peripancreatic lymph nodes were noted (up to 0.9 cm). MRI abdomen with MRCP corroborated these findings, confirming SMA contact of approximately 170 degrees and SMV narrowing without occlusion, with a patent main portal vein. No hepatic metastases were identified on MRI.

Endoscopic ultrasound (EUS)-guided fine needle biopsy of the pancreatic head mass was performed. Pathology demonstrated moderately differentiated pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (PDAC). Immunohistochemistry was positive for CK7, CK19, MUC1, and CA 19-9, and negative for CK20 and CDX2, confirming pancreatobiliary origin and excluding metastatic colorectal adenocarcinoma, acinar cell carcinoma, and neuroendocrine tumor. Ki-67 proliferation index was approximately 35%, consistent with moderate proliferative activity. Lymphovascular invasion was not identified on the biopsy specimen.

The case was referred to our multidisciplinary pancreatic tumor board for consensus treatment planning. This note represents the medical oncology consultation from that tumor board discussion.

---

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY

1. Type 2 diabetes mellitus — currently diet-controlled; no insulin or oral hypoglycemics at baseline
2. Hypertension — on amlodipine
3. Obstructive jaundice secondary to pancreatic head adenocarcinoma — as above
4. No prior abdominal surgeries
5. No prior history of pancreatitis or pancreatic cysts
6. No prior malignancy

---

SOCIAL HISTORY

Ms. Osei-Bonsu is a retired librarian of Ghanaian descent. She is widowed and lives independently. She reports a 15-pack-year smoking history and quit approximately 10 years ago. She denies current alcohol use. She lives with her adult daughter nearby, who accompanies her to appointments and will be a key support person during treatment. She is highly motivated and engaged in her care, having researched her diagnosis extensively prior to today's visit.

---

FAMILY HISTORY

No known family history of pancreatic cancer. No family history of breast, ovarian, colon, or other gastrointestinal malignancies. Mother died of cardiovascular disease in her 80s. Father died of stroke. Two siblings alive and well. Germline testing will be recommended given the diagnosis of PDAC; however, family history is not suggestive of a hereditary syndrome at this time.

---

ALLERGIES

No known drug allergies.

---

MEDICATIONS

1. Amlodipine 5 mg orally once daily
2. Ondansetron 8 mg orally as needed for nausea (recent prescription from PCP)
3. Prochlorperazine 10 mg orally as needed for breakthrough nausea
4. No anticoagulation
5. No antiplatelet agents
6. No metformin or insulin (diabetes diet-controlled)

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

Positive for: progressive jaundice, pruritus, dark urine, pale/greasy stools, 14-pound unintentional weight loss, epigastric pain radiating to the back, fatigue, reduced appetite, and early satiety.

Negative for: fever, chills, hematemesis, melena, hematochezia, chest pain, dyspnea, lower extremity edema, new neurological symptoms, skin rash, or joint pain.

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

Vital signs: Blood pressure 128/76 mmHg, heart rate 82 bpm, respiratory rate 16/min, temperature 98.4°F, SpO2 98% on room air, weight 148 lbs (BMI 24.5, down from an estimated baseline of ~162 lbs per PCP records).

General: Alert, oriented, well-groomed woman in no acute distress. Mild icteric appearance of skin. Visibly fatigued but conversational and engaged.

HEENT: Scleral icterus present bilaterally. Oropharynx clear. No lymphadenopathy in cervical or supraclavicular chains.

Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops. No jugular venous distension.

Respiratory: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezes or rhonchi.

Abdomen: Soft, mildly tender to deep palpation in the epigastric and right upper quadrant regions. A palpable, non-tender gallbladder is noted beneath the right costal margin (consistent with Courvoisier sign, already documented). No peritoneal signs. Mild hepatomegaly on percussion. Bowel sounds are present and active. No ascites on clinical exam.

Extremities: No peripheral edema. No palpable masses.

Skin: Mild jaundice. No scratch marks from pruritus noted on exam. No spider angiomata or palmar erythema.

Neurological: Grossly intact. No focal deficits.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS

Complete blood count: WBC 7.2 x10^9/L, hemoglobin 11.8 g/dL (mild anemia of chronic disease), platelets 264 x10^9/L.

Comprehensive metabolic panel: Total bilirubin 8.2 mg/dL, direct bilirubin 6.9 mg/dL, alkaline phosphatase 420 U/L, AST 98 U/L, ALT 112 U/L, GGT 318 U/L, albumin 3.1 g/dL, total protein 6.4 g/dL, creatinine 0.82 mg/dL, BUN 14 mg/dL, glucose 118 mg/dL (mildly elevated, consistent with known diabetes and stress response).

Coagulation: PT/INR 1.1. PTT within normal limits.

Tumor markers: CA 19-9 1,204 U/mL (markedly elevated; upper limit of normal 37 U/mL), CEA 7.1 ng/mL (mildly elevated).

Note: The patient has no known Gilbert syndrome; Lewis antigen status has not been formally assessed, though very high CA 19-9 in this clinical context is highly consistent with malignancy rather than a false-positive elevation.

---

IMAGING RESULTS

Pancreatic protocol CT abdomen/pelvis (reviewed at tumor board): 3.8 cm hypoenhancing mass in the pancreatic head and uncinate process. Main pancreatic duct dilation to 7 mm upstream. Common bile duct dilated to 16 mm with abrupt cutoff at the level of the pancreatic head, consistent with malignant obstruction. The mass abuts the SMA over approximately 180 degrees of its circumference and involves the SMV with focal narrowing. No encasement of the celiac axis or proper hepatic artery. No hepatic lesions. No peritoneal implants. Two peripancreatic lymph nodes measuring up to 0.9 cm — suspicious for nodal involvement given size and location, though formally sub-centimeter. Classified as borderline resectable by AHPBA/SSO/SSAT criteria based on SMA abutment exceeding 180 degrees and SMV involvement.

MRI abdomen with MRCP: Corroborates the CT findings. SMA contact estimated at approximately 170 degrees. SMV narrowing present but without complete occlusion; main portal vein is patent. No hepatic lesions. No ascites. Main pancreatic duct 6.5 mm in the body proximal to the obstruction. No distant metastatic disease identified.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

Ms. Osei-Bonsu is a 66-year-old woman with a new diagnosis of borderline resectable pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma of the pancreatic head and uncinate process, clinical stage T3 N1 M0 (AJCC 8th edition, Stage IIB). Her disease is classified as borderline resectable based on SMA abutment of approximately 170–180 degrees and SMV involvement with focal luminal narrowing, per AHPBA/SSO/SSAT criteria. She has biopsy-proven moderately differentiated PDAC with CK7/CK19/MUC1-positive immunoprofile. Her CA 19-9 is markedly elevated at 1,204 U/mL. Performance status is ECOG 1. She currently has significant biliary obstruction with bilirubin 8.2 mg/dL that will need to be addressed prior to systemic chemotherapy.

The case was discussed in detail at our multidisciplinary pancreatic tumor board today, attended by hepatobiliary surgery, medical oncology, radiation oncology, interventional gastroenterology (endoscopy), and diagnostic radiology. The following consensus recommendations were made:

1. BILIARY DECOMPRESSION: The patient will be referred urgently to interventional gastroenterology for ERCP with placement of a fully covered self-expanding metallic biliary stent (FCSEMS). Stent placement is recommended over plastic stents for durability, given that the anticipated neoadjuvant course will be several months in duration. Biliary decompression is necessary to safely administer chemotherapy — the current bilirubin of 8.2 mg/dL would preclude safe dosing of Camptosar and impair clearance of multiple chemotherapy agents. We expect bilirubin to normalize within 2–3 weeks following successful stent placement. Repeat LFTs will be checked at 2 weeks post-ERCP before chemotherapy initiation.

2. NEOADJUVANT CHEMOTHERAPY WITH MODIFIED FOLFIRINOX: Following biliary decompression and normalization of liver function tests, the patient will initiate neoadjuvant chemotherapy with a modified FOLFIRINOX regimen. This will consist of oxali 85 mg/m2, leucovorin 400 mg/m2, iri 150 mg/m2 (reduced from the standard 180 mg/m2 given the presence of a biliary stent and a mild concern regarding Gilbert syndrome-like physiology given her mildly elevated bilirubin history — we will reassess Camptosar dosing after cycle 1 based on LFT normalization), and Adrucil 2400 mg/m2 as a 46-hour continuous infusion, administered every 14 days. Treatment intent is curative/neoadjuvant. Eight cycles (approximately 16 weeks) are planned. FOLFIRINOX was selected over gem/nab-pac based on her relatively young age, good performance status (ECOG 1), and the data from the PRODIGE 4/ACCORD 11 trial and subsequent neoadjuvant PDAC literature supporting superior tumor downstaging with FOLFIRINOX in fit patients with borderline resectable disease.

   Granulocyte colony-stimulating factor (G-CSF) support with Neulasta (pegfilgrastim) will be administered with each cycle of FOLFIRINOX given the known risk of febrile neutropenia with this regimen.

   The goals of neoadjuvant therapy include: (a) potential downstaging of the SMA abutment to less than 90 degrees to allow safer margin-negative resection; (b) early treatment of potential micrometastatic disease; (c) use of the neoadjuvant period as a biological test — patients who develop disease progression during neoadjuvant therapy are spared the morbidity of a Whipple procedure that would not have been beneficial.

3. RESTAGING: Restaging CT with pancreatic protocol will be performed after cycles 4 and 8. CA 19-9 will be checked after every 2 cycles. If CA 19-9 fails to decline or imaging demonstrates disease progression, the treatment plan will be reassessed, including consideration of alternative chemotherapy regimens or palliative intent.

4. CONSIDERATION OF CHEMORADIATION AND SURGERY: Following completion of planned neoadjuvant chemotherapy, restaging will be performed. If the patient demonstrates continued disease control or response, we will reassess resectability. The tumor board has noted that consolidation chemoradiation (long-course with a radiosensitizing fluoropyrimidine, or SBRT) prior to surgical resection may be considered as an additional step to further sterilize the surgical margin, particularly the SMA margin, before Whipple procedure. Surgical eligibility will be formally reassessed at that time by hepatobiliary surgery.

5. GERMLINE TESTING: Germline genetic testing for hereditary pancreatic cancer syndromes (including BRCA1/2, PALB2, ATM, MLH1/MSH2/MSH6/PMS2/EPCAM, CDKN2A, and STK11) has been ordered today via a blood-based germline panel. Results are pending. Germline testing in all newly diagnosed PDAC patients is now standard per NCCP guidelines, and results could influence both family counseling and therapeutic eligibility (e.g., PARP inhibitor maintenance if a BRCA2 mutation is found).

6. NUTRITIONAL AND SUPPORTIVE CARE: The patient has lost 14 pounds and has an albumin of 3.1 g/dL, indicating significant nutritional compromise. Referral to oncology dietitian has been placed. She has exocrine insufficiency-like symptoms (greasy stools) secondary to pancreatic duct obstruction and may benefit from a trial of pancreatic enzyme replacement therapy (PERT) with meals. Palliative care has been co-introduced as a supportive care resource, which the patient accepted. Diabetes management will be monitored throughout chemotherapy given her diet-controlled type 2 diabetes and the potential for blood glucose fluctuations on a dexamethasone-containing antiemetic regimen.

7. PORT PLACEMENT: Given the planned 8 cycles of intravenous chemotherapy involving continuous 5-FU infusion via ambulatory pump, placement of a tunneled central venous access device (port-a-cath) is recommended. Referral to interventional radiology for port placement has been placed, to be performed after biliary stent placement and prior to cycle 1.

8. PATIENT COUNSELING: Extensive time was spent discussing the diagnosis, treatment rationale, goals of neoadjuvant therapy, and realistic expectations regarding resectability. The patient was counseled that even after an excellent response to chemotherapy, surgical resection is not guaranteed — it will depend on reassessment of vascular involvement. However, the patient expressed a very clear, strong desire to pursue aggressive curative-intent therapy. She was accompanied by her daughter and both asked thoughtful questions. Informed consent for neoadjuvant FOLFIRINOX was obtained and documented. The patient was given written patient education materials and scheduled for a return visit in 2 weeks following ERCP for chemotherapy planning.

Returning faculty: Medical Oncology (attending), Hepatobiliary Surgery (attending), Radiation Oncology (attending), Interventional Gastroenterology, Diagnostic Radiology.

Next appointment: Medical oncology follow-up in 2 weeks, post-ERCP LFTs to be reviewed prior to chemotherapy initiation.

Electronically signed by: Attending Medical Oncologist, Multidisciplinary Pancreatic Oncology Program.
